responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hidden Gifts: The Mystery of Angus MacPhee. A film by Nick Higgins Icarus Films, 2005, 25 minutes.

Sander Gilman

Sander Gilman

S420 Callaway Center
Atlanta, GA 30322-0660
E-mail: sander34@aol.com

Sander Gilman  

“Art brut,” or as one of those interviewed in this film, the art therapist Joyce Laing, calls it, “art extraordinary,” is an obsession of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries that has become a commonplace of the twenty-first. Who is any longer surprised by the naive, criminal, or mentally ill individual who produces art (and the galleries in which we find it)? The theme of such art, and the stated interpretation in this short and elegant film, is that “art brut” is created by short-circuiting the pathways between the unconscious and the work of art: such artists simply create, just as we simply dream. Our dreams are amorphous and are retained only in crude approximation in our narratives when we awake; these artists articulate in an unmediated manner the creative impulse. The film quite correctly argues that not all such artists are mentally ill and that not all of the mentally ill show creative impulses and abilities.

The case of the crofter Angus MacPhee (or McPhee: sources have it both ways), from South Uist in the Western Isles off Scotland, is an excellent case for the much more complex reading of both art and “art brut” in a short film that is self-consciously aware of the difficulties of both categories. MacPhee was invalided out of the British army in 1940 as someone unable to serve. In 1940 (after Dunkirk) this meant that he was already seriously compromised.

405

responseSet.title: “My Story for my Better Self”: Love, Loss, and Working Through in the Diary of Elizabeth Barrett Browning
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“My Story for my Better Self”: Love, Loss, and Working Through in the Diary of Elizabeth Barrett Browning

Wendy Katz

Wendy Katz

262 Central Park West, 1B
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: wk172@columbia.edu

Wendy Katz   Faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

An approach to the diary as more than a private record, but a psychologically meaningful act and object, is demonstrated by a close reading of the diary of the English poet Elizabeth Barrett Browning. Attending to both formal and thematic aspects, this study attempts to illustrate the variable uses of diary-keeping, as well as to identify its uses of specific importance to this particular diarist. The more expansive and profound insight thus gained into the poet's life and work points to the potential of a psychoanalytic approach to such uniquely intimate documents. Evidence is offered to support the position that Browning's diary served important integrating functions during a critical period of her life: modulating overwhelming affect, organizing experiences beyond her control, and serving as a concrete symbol of preservation in the face of accumulative painful losses. The diary-keeping was a self-therapeutic endeavor, mediating and metabolizing these losses into a work of mourning as the poet elaborated and memorialized them.

Announcing the JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA is pleased to announce a new annual prize to recognize significant contributions to the journal by new authors. The New Author Prize will be awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and will include a $500 honorarium to be presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author will be considered someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. The editor will establish a mentoring/editorial group to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the usual JAPA submission process using the online system accessed at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, an author may indicate in the cover letter that the manuscript is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that the paper be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication will remain with the editorial board and the editor-in-chief, as will the decision about selecting the annual prize-winning paper.

responseSet.title: Introduction: Creativity, Psychopathology, and Clinical Theory
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Creativity, Psychopathology, and Clinical Theory

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

E-mail: belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

Psychoanalysis has been fascinated with questions surrounding creativity. In his papers on creativity and on specific artists, Freud set in motion a discourse that has been taken up by many writers ever since. Traditionally, psychoanalytic theories tried to include exceptional capacities and persons within the same conceptual frameworks established to explain routine (i.e., normal and deviant) behaviors—unconscious and (pre)conscious processes; the pleasure and reality principles; primary and secondary processes; defenses such as repression, sublimation, and dissociation. If these concepts and processes are able to distinguish one pathology from another (e.g., neurosis from psychosis, hysterias from obsessive disorders), then why not apply them to explain what distinguishes the creative artist from the rest of us?

Consequently there is a considerable psychoanalytic literature on the relation of creativity to psychodynamic concepts that asks questions such as the following. Is the artist able to dip into deeper (i.e., unconscious) levels of the psyche, utilize more primary processes dominated by the pleasure principle, but still not lose a sense of reality to which he or she returns, with artistic product in hand? Do we respond to those creative products because they provide us a safe connection to an underground (i.e., unconscious, primary process) part of ourselves? Still another focus in the psychoanalytic literature has been to question the connection between the artist's psychopathology and his or her ability to produce creative works: How interdependent are creativity and personality structure? If one “cures” the pathology, will creative capacity disappear or be liberated? Does the artist's fluid motility between fantasy and reality make him or her more (or less) prone to mental illness?

In recent years psychoanalytic theories have increasingly emphasized the functions of other persons—intrapsychically, interpersonally, or intersubjectively—in the development of psychic structure. From the perspectives of these theories, the examination of creativity becomes centered on the artist's interactions with significant others, especially early on in life.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Listening with A Different Theory: Commentary on Skorczewski

Kenneth Newman

Kenneth Newman

122 South Michigan Avenue, 13th Floor
Chicago, IL 60603

Kenneth Newman   Faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

In his first book on the narcissistic personality, Kohut (1971) said that the determining event that caused him to rethink his classical position was a shrillness in his patient Miss F.'s voice. Repeatedly she objected to his interpretations, which focused mainly on his belief that her central conflict was oedipal and that her transference to him was an eroticized paternal one. But what she seemed most distressed by was his insistence on recasting her associations into the language of drive and defense, with Kohut the principal target of her fantasies. She loudly proclaimed that when he altered her presentations to fit his theory he was undermining the analysis. Something about her tone led him to listen in a different way—that is, in accordance with her insistence that he be more of a mirror. What he would learn from Miss F. would serve as an antidote for her weakly cathected and injured self. Kohut was able to hear in Miss F.'s communications that the tendrils of a narcissistic transference need not be a resistance, thus ushering in a major change in how we might understand many patients' symptoms, compromised object relations, and character pathology.

Viewing a patient's presenting psychopathology as revealing disturbances that could be traced back to early environmental failures meant that major traumas were linked to failures by caretakers who had been necessary for building psychic structure. The narcissistic transferences communicating the weakness of the self expressed and carried forward these unmet needs for objects (selfobjects) that would be vital in rehabilitation. These narcissistic transferences (mirror, twinship, alter ego, or idealizing) are not then pressing for the fulfillment of infantile wishes, nor are they indicative of the ego's defense against drive-centered conflicts; rather, they represent the emergence of heretofore repressed or walled-off needs. Most important, when these transferences become mobilized in treatment and the patient acknowledges the role the therapist is being assigned, it is a sign of progress.

[[RunningHead]]

B. Stanley & L.J. Siever (2010). The interpersonal dimension of borderline personality disorder: Toward a neuropeptide model.Am. J. Psychiatry167:24-39.

Jeffrey Erbe and Eric Fertuck

Eric Fertuck

Eric Fertuck  

In a review article published in the American Journal of Psychiatry, Barbara Stanley and Lawrence Siever introduce a new, cogent, and reasonably comprehensive neurobiological model of borderline personality disorder (BPD). This model links self-injurious behavior and interpersonal difficulties in BPD patients to neuropeptide activity. The researchers argue that dysregulated opioid, oxytocin, and vasopressin neuropeptide systems contribute to behavioral and affective dysfunction and interpersonal problems for individuals with BPD. The model is comprehensive in that it also aims to elucidate the unstable relationships, feelings of emptiness, and other experiences of patients with BPD.

Many studies have documented the involvement of endogenous opioids in mediating physical and emotional pain. Stanley and Siever posit that opioids also play a role in the experience of negative emotions and self-injurious behavior, which are common features of BPD. Support for this hypothesis comes from studies demonstrating low baseline opioid levels in limbic circuitry (e.g., cingulate cortex and amygdala) and increased opioid receptors throughout the brain in individuals with BPD. The authors propose that altered opioid functioning may relate to the chronic mental pain, lack of a sense of well-being, sensitivity to rejection, and difficulties in self-soothing that are associated with BPD.

580

[[RunningHead]]

C.A. Webb, R.J. DeRubeis, & J.P. Barber. (2010). Therapist adherence-competence and treatment outcome: A meta-analytic review.Journal of Consulting & Clinical Psychology78:200-211.

Kevin McCarthy and Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford  

Any type of psychotherapy, psychoanalysis included, consists of a number of interventions that clinicians expect will help patients improve. Greater use of these techniques is considered “adherence” to a theory or manual of treatment. Selective and timely application of these techniques is considered “competence” at delivering the treatment. It is reasonable to assume that when clinicians are more adherent to the techniques put forth in a treatment and are more competent at delivering the techniques, patients should improve more than when these conditions are not met. This principle strongly guides psychoanalytic education, which emphasizes adherence to core techniques of psychoanalysis in order to provide maximum clinical benefit and to demarcate psychoanalysis from related treatment modalities. However, a recent study of adherence-competence measures and their relation to psychotherapy outcome performed by Webb, DeRubeis, and Barber (2010) suggests that this assumption may not be correct.

Webb and colleagues reviewed electronic databases and reference sections of papers dealing with adherence-competence to find empirical investigations (published and unpublished) of individual psychotherapy that included at least five clinical patients and used an observer-rated measure of adherence-competence. Of the 5,868 articles returned by this search, 36 met all the criteria.

977

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mirrors of Memory: Freud, Photography, and the History of Art. By Mary Bergstein. Ithaca: Cornell University Press, 2010, 335 pp., $29.96.

Ellen Spitz

Ellen Spitz

1000 Hilltop Circle
Baltimore, MD 21250
E-mail: spitz@umbc.edu

Ellen Spitz  

Discomforted by the stifling atmosphere of an oppressive Parisian heat wave, Ellen Handler Spitz awoke one morning to find her understanding of the origins of psychoanalysis transformed by an erudite and original book that revealed to her the far-reaching effects of European fin de siècle visual culture on Freud's mind. This book, Mirrors of Memory, by Mary Bergstein, illuminates the heretofore unexamined ways in which the medium of photography, widely taken to be a transparent, objective way of documenting and gaining access to a previously existing reality, was relied on by many disciplines during Freud's lifetime. Among these disciplines were archaeology, ethnology, psychiatry and neurology, art connoisseurship, criminology, and travel literature. As such, photography interpenetrated the cognitive styles of the period and shaped Freud's thinking in terms of both content and method. There, in one fell swoop, you have it.

Before addressing the content of this extraordinary book, Mirrors of Memory, I want to extol it first as a paragon in the annals of interdisciplinary scholarship. A model of excellence, it could well serve as a recommended text for authors aspiring to similar projects.

Cross-disciplinary work of distinction, modeled by the book under review here, entails a voyage that brooks no shortcuts. It reveals an authorial voice sustained by strong motivation, fired by imagination and curiosity, honed by a first-rate education, buoyed by tenacity, and seasoned with equal measures of humility and daring. It is work that provides rich opportunities for intellectual growth and for amazing discoveries. Bergstein reminds me all the way through of one of my seminars in graduate school with the distinguished Renaissance art historian James Ackerman at Harvard. Fierce and imposing, he enjoined us on our memorable first day of class to submit no paper that included even a single sentence without a citation. Any sentence without a footnote, he warned, would be excised. We were being trained as serious researchers: callow speculation was deemed worthless. At the time, I was mortified. Mary Bergstein, however, has managed to adhere almost literally to Ackerman's severe decree (all right, not every sentence of her book is footnoted, but the range and specificity of her citations are truly formidable)

Announcing the JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA is pleased to announce a new annual prize to recognize significant contributions to the journal by new authors. The New Author Prize will be awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and will include a $500 honorarium to be presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author will be considered someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. The editor will establish a mentoring/editorial group to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the usual JAPA submission process using the online system accessed at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, an author may indicate in the cover letter that the manuscript is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that the paper be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication will remain with the editorial board and the editor-in-chief, as will the decision about selecting the annual prize-winning paper.

Interaction between the serotonin transporter gene (5-HTTLPR), stressful life events, and risk of depression: A meta-analysis.Journal of the American Medical Association301:2462-2471.

Aaron Krasner , Bret Rutherford and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

Steven Roose  

A fundamental tenet of psychoanalytic theory and technique is that experiences in early life (internal and external) strongly affect all aspects of mental life, but supporting this hypothesis with other than clinical data has proved elusive. However, recent technological advancements in molecular

137

genetics have allowed investigators to undertake innovative studies of how genes interact with environmental characteristics to produce an observed phenotype (i.e., gene-environment [g x e] interactions).

Perhaps the most influential study demonstrating a gene-environment interaction was by Caspi et al. (2003). This study found that genetic variations in the promoter region of the serotonin transporter gene (5-HTTLPR) play a role in predisposition to major depression. The authors postulated that the short allele (s) of this gene confers deficient serotonergic response to stressful life events, whereas the long allele (1) is associated with an effective serotonergic response. Women were classified as s/s, s/l, l/s, or l/l and were asked to report on childhood abuse. Women with the s/s genotype had high rates of depression if they had suffered significant childhood trauma. Those without such childhood stressors did not show a high rate of depression, even if they had the s/s genotype. Conversely, patients with the l/l genotype (good serotonin response) did not become depressed at a higher rate, irrespective of the presence or absence of childhood trauma. Thus, the genotype interacts with the environment to either predispose individuals to depression in adult life or to protect them from it. This study was considered groundbreaking, and the expectation was that more g x e interactions would be demonstrated for schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and even personality disorders.

However, a critical test of the validity of any resarch finding is whether it is replicated. In the case of the Caspi et al. study, only four of thirteen subsequent studies replicated the authors' findings. While a metaanalysis is not a substitute for a well-conducted study, it can be a useful method for evaluating apparently conflicting study results. A recent JAMA article (Risch et al. 2009) used metaanalytic techniques to determine whether an interaction between serotonin transporter polymorphisms and stressful life events in fact confers an increased risk of major depression. An extensive literature search resulted in the identification of fourteen studies, enrolling 14,250 participants (1,769 with depression, 12,481 without), that included data on stressful life events, 5-HTTLPR genotype, and depression. The authors devised a standardized coding system for the presence or absence of depression and stressful life events that allowed a uniform approach to the data presented in the different studies. The metaanalysis documented an association between the number of stressful life events and depression. Subjects with a greater number of stressful life events had an incrementally higher risk of depression (odds ratio [OR] 1.31 for 1 vs. 0 events, OR 1.95 for 2 vs. 0 events, and OR 3.21 for >2 vs. 0 events) compared to subjects with fewer stressful life events.

The Struggle against Mourning. By Ilany Kogan. Lanham, MD: Jason Aronson, 2007, 263 pp., $39.95.

Brenda Solomon

Brenda Solomon

150 Park Avenue
Glencoe, IL 60022
E-mail: brsolomon@comcast.net

Brenda Solomon  

In opening a collection that often reads like a good novel, Ilany Kogan writes that “this book has grown out of my own struggle against mourning, on the professional as well as on the personal level. It illustrates the pathway from absence of mourning to an emotional awareness of pain and loss, including the therapeutic dilemmas involved in such a journey” (p. vii). Kogan is a training analyst with the Israel Psychoanalytic Society and chief supervisor of the Psychotherapy Center of the Treatment of the Child and Adolescent in Bucharest. She is a member of the scientific advisory board of the Fritz Bauer Institute for Holocaust Studies in Frankfurt.

Her analytic interests are shaped by the fact that she shares a common traumatic historical experience with many of her analysands. She belongs to that large group of adults whose parents were affected, directly or indirectly, by the Holocaust. In her published personal history (Kogan 2003) she recalls her father, who on completing his studies in pediatric medicine in Vienna returned to Romania, where he married Kogan's mother.

Flights of Fancy

The Brightening Glance: Imagination and Childhood. By Ellen Handler Spitz. New York: Pantheon Books, 2006, 250 pp., $25.00 hardcover, $14.95 paperback.

Lynn Reiser

Lynn Reiser

255 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: lynnreiser@comcast.net

Lynn Reiser  

This delightful book, which takes its title from Yeats's “Among School Children” (1928), addresses children's very early experiences with art and demonstrates how the adults who love them can facilitate growth and learning “in their sensory, perceptual, and imaginative lives” (p. 4). Intended as a book for a general audience comprising parents, teachers, librarians, and others closely involved with children, it is surely of interest to psychoanalysts as well.

Handler Spitz began with an “idea of writing a book about first experiences in the traditional arts such as dance, theater, music, literature, and visual art”; she “came to see that children do not separate these out … from other aspects of their lives—from their ordinary excursions, their living spaces, and play spaces, and their holidays” (p. 5). She brings her wide-ranging and unique interests and experiences to bear upon these topics, with chapters on children's relationships with caregivers, music, favorite possessions, birthday parties, and rooms. She also considers how children deal with death, and the effect of childhood aesthetic experiences on their futures as adults.

In each chapter she weaves together various themes, as well as different narrative voices: former child, “artist, dancer, writer, parent and scholar” (p. 4). She uses her expertise in art history and aesthetics, research training in psychoanalysis, and appreciation of popular culture to elucidate “all manner of stories, objects, plays, songs, [and] film” (p. 4), giving extended close readings of several works of art, including an episode of the television program Mr. Rogers' Neighborhood, the comic strip Calvin and Hobbes, a children's picture book, A Birthday for Frances, Keats's “La Belle Dame sans Merci” (1819), and anecdotes from her own childhood and the lives of her children and those of her friends.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: How Should Psychoanalysis Adapt to Certain Challenges of Contemporary Culture?

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

E-mail: rmgottlied@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

The two papers that follow—one by Thomas Ogden and Glen Gabbard, the other by Lena Ehrlich—suggest adaptations of psychoanalytic technique to current challenges faced by our profession. Ogden and Gabbard observe that “there is currently intense pressure within contemporary Western culture for rapid, efficient, inexpensive, and painless solutions to … psychological problems” (p. 534). They add that “the contemporary therapeutic culture … places a premium on symptomatic ‘cure,’” and that “analysts are vulnerable to succumbing to… pressures for the achievement of symptomatic improvement” (p. 533).

Ehrlich responds to another set of contemporary pressures, those brought on by the current financial crisis. Today, she writes, “patients are facing real and/or imagined financial adversities that lead them to rationalize or justify their fears about beginning or continuing analysis” (p. 515).

Cassius-like, the authors of both papers counsel that the fault may be “not in our stars, but in ourselves.” If we find that insufficient symptomatic improvement has become a central issue in an analysis, it is wise to reflect on the possible ways this issue may be functioning within ourselves—that is, in the countertransference. And if, citing financial exigencies or worries, a patient insists on changes to the analytic frame (fee, frequency of sessions, termination), our impulse toward ready agreement in the light of the financial crisis ought to evoke our analytic curiosity, especially about our own experience of this analysis—that is, about the countertransference.

Each of these papers identifies a contemporary cultural pressure that may militate against analysis as it is usually practiced—that is, without our primary attention being riveted on the relief of symptoms and with a certain frequency, continuity, and fee. These authors propose we not forget that our emotions are entangled in the treatment along with our patients'.

[[RunningHead]]

Dynamic Deconstructive Psychotherapy for Borderline Personality Disorder Comorbid with Alcohol use Disorders: 30-Month Follow-up

Robert Gregory , Evan DeLucia-Deranja and Jacqueline Mogle

Jacqueline Mogle

750 East Adams Street
Syracuse, NY 13210
E-mail: gregoryr@upstate.edu

Jacqueline Mogle  

Patients having co-occurring borderline personality disorder and alcohol use disorders constitute a common but particularly severe and refractory subgroup. A recent randomized controlled trial (Gregory and Remen 2008) employed dynamic deconstructive psychotherapy (DDP), a time-limited, manual-based weekly individual treatment. Thirty participants with co-occurring alcohol use disorders and borderline personality disorder (BPD) received 12 months of either DDP or optimized community care (OCC). DDP resulted in significant decreases in heavy drinking days, parasuicide episodes, and institutional care, as well as significant improvements in core symptoms of BPD, depression, and perceived social support (Gregory et al. 2008). Although the results of this trial were promising, the long-term outcomes are not known. The present study directly addresses this question by reporting on the 30-month outcomes of the participants in that trial and testing the hypothesis that treatment gains achieved during treatment with DDP will be sustained after treatment ends.

A. Bateman & P. Fonagy (2009). Randomized controlled trial of outpatient mentalization-based treatment versus structured clinical management for borderline personality disorder.Am. J. Psychiatry166:1355-1364.

Aaron Krasner

Aaron Krasner

Aaron Krasner  

Peter Fonagy and Anthony Bateman have published the results of a prospective randomized controlled trial comparing mentalization-based treatment (MBT) to structured clinical management (SCM) for borderline personality disorder (BPD). Patients treated with MBT had fewer suicide attempts, episodes of severe self-injury, and psychiatric hospitalizations during the eighteen months of treatment compared with patients treated with SCM. Before a discussion of this finding, some background on mentalization may be useful. Mentalization is the capacity to meaningfully reflect on one's own and other people's mental states (Fonagy 2000). Mentalization is a hybrid concept derived from Wilfred Bion's discussion of containment in the infant-mother dyad, attachment theory, and cognitive theory. Fonagy and Bateman have have used mentalization as a conceptual framework to develop a manualized treatment for BPD in a variety of clinical settings. MBT focuses on mental states and fosters attachment contexts that in turn enhance patients' capacity to mentalize.

In the current trial, 134 outpatients referred for treatment to a specialist personality disorder service were randomly assigned to either MBT or SCM. Inclusion criteria were diagnosis of BPD (DSM-IV criteria), recent suicide attempt or episode of self-harm, and age 18-65. Exclusion criteria were active engagement in long-term psychotherapy, psychotic or bipolar I disorders, opiate dependence requiring specialist treatment, or organic brain disorder. The authors describe MBT as “manualized, consisting of 18 months of weekly combined individual and group psychotherapy provided by two different therapists” and SCM as “regular individual and group sessions … based on a counseling model closest to a supportive approach.” Patients were allowed to continue or begin psychotropic medications, and treatment was provided by a study psychiatrist blind to the psychotherapy assignment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Falling behind and Catching up with Contemporary Research on Personality Disorders: Introduction to Lenzenweger

Eric Fertuck

Eric Fertuck

E-mail: ef304@columbia.edu

Eric Fertuck  

There have been several clarion calls in the history of American psychoanalysis regarding the need for greater and more sophisticated empirical research. One came in 1981, when the American Psychoanalytic Association commissioned a joint task force on careers in psychoanalytic research. Philip Holzman, a psychoanalyst and experimental psychopathologist who directed a pioneering psychopathology laboratory at Harvard for much of his career (until his death in 2004), chaired this task force. He was one of only a few clinical psychologists to receive full psychoanalytic training within an APsaA-accredited institute in the 1950s. Holzman and his task force colleagues concluded that psychoanalysis “requires the nourishment which comes from those who devote themselves essentially full-time to the research effort. Without this group, psychoanalysis is in danger of falling behind in its scientific status” (quoted in Holzman and Perry 1986, p. 179; emphasis added). An outgrowth of this recommendation was a panel co-chaired by Holzman and J. Christopher Perry, held at the fall meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association in 1983, titled “Borderline Personality Disorders (BPD): Research Issues and New Empirical Findings.” This panel coincided with the first DSM manual to formally recognize BPD and personality disorders more generally, according them their own axis and diagnostic research criteria.

Twenty-seven years later, the following contribution by Mark Lenzenweger—a former postdoctoral fellow in Holzman's lab—is a testament to the enormous growth in empirical knowledge of personality disorders (PDs) and BPD since that time. This review invites us to revisit the warning of Holzman and colleagues and ask ourselves anew, Has psychoanalysis fallen behind in the empirical study of PDs?

737

Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford  

Placebo effects have long been considered nuisances in psychiatry, since their presence can complicate efforts to ascertain the biological mechanisms and efficacies of medication and psychotherapy. However, this view has changed in recent years with increasing recognition of placebo effects' utility in understanding the functioning of various normal brain systems, in elucidating the pathways by which effective treatments for psychiatric disorders may work, and in facilitating the clinical treatment of patients. A new publication by Lidstone and colleagues (2010) adds to the growing literature on placebo effects by demonstrating how patient expectancy leads to physiological changes that ameliorate the symptoms of Parkinson's disease (PD).

An influential earlier publication in Science by the same research group was among the first to report an objective basis for placebo effects. Before this work, it was not known whether the observed effects of placebos on a target disorder represented an actual improvement in the disorder or instead reflected a response bias. Based on response biases, patients may report symptom improvement even when the severity of their disorder is unchanged. De la Fuente-Fernandez et al. (2001) reported the results of a positron emission tomography (PET) study in which six subjects with PD were given placebo or active medication and asked to rate their symptoms. Dopamine release in the striatum was estimated with PET based on the competition between endogenous dopamine and [11C] raclopride (RAC) for binding to dopamine receptors.

975

Kevin McCarthy

Kevin McCarthy

Kevin McCarthy  

Many have considered the ultimate goal of psychoanalysis to be personality change, though we often expect symptom change will occur as well. Historically, pharmacological treatment has been conceptualized as providing symptom relief so that patients can more fully engage in therapy; less frequently have analysts believed these medications will fulfill the goal of personality change. However, a new study, by T. Z. Tang and colleagues, suggests that antidepressant medication may lead to personality as well as symptom change in depressed patients, and that greater personality change while on medication may prevent relapse into depression.

Patients in treatment outcome studies frequently show changes in personality following a course of antidepressant medication, but in connection with symptom changes. The common explanation for this correlation between changes in symptoms and in personality has been the state-effect hypothesis: when in the state of depression, patients ascribe more negative personality traits to themselves; when they feel better, they feel less need to report those same characteristics.

582

The Crisis in Psychoanalysis: In Search of a Lost Doctrine. By Ahmed Fayek. Austin, TX: Bridgeway Books, 2010, 282 pp., $49.95.

Dale Boesky

Dale Boesky

614 Watkins Street
Birmingham, MI 48009-1470
E-mail: dboesky@comcast.net

Dale Boesky  

A jeremiad is a literary work in which the author, like the Old Testament prophet, bitterly laments the state of society. It often contains a prophecy that the end of something is near—in the present instance, the end of psychoanalysis. In The Crisis in Psychoanalysis, Ahmed Fayek concludes that the dire situation reflected in our present theoretical pluralism could have been forestalled had Freud (and we who came after) stuck to his original “classical model,” by which Fayek means the topographic hypothesis that mapped the systems ucs, pcs, and cs. He warns us sternly that the time remaining for us to remedy this error is running out. This appears to him to be a weltuntergangen danger. One of his principal arguments here is that Freud never properly understood the difference between functional and structural entities, and that his abandonment of the system ucs was unwarranted. Functional entities are a conceptual dead end, but structural entities capture the essence of the system ucs, for Fayek Freud's most profound insight into the human mind. Thus, he concludes that Freud's 1923 monograph The Ego and the Id has proven to be a fateful blunder. He calls the structural hypothesis that Freud introduced in that monograph the “replacement” topography, fatally flawed because it omits the system ucs.

From the Editor

Comings and Goings

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

It is again time to extend our gratitude to those whose terms on the Editorial Board came to a close on December 31. We say good-bye with thanks to Lee Ascherman, Judith F. Chused, Peter Dunn, Dianne Elise, Gerald I. Fogel, Peter Goldberg, Linda C. Mayes, Donald Moss, and Richard Munich. We bid farewell also to Karin Ahbel-Rappe, Ayelet Barkai, Christine C. Kieffer, Adele Tutter, and Jeanine M. Vivona, our Editorial Associates. This outgoing group of board members and associates did an extraordinary amount of impressive work during their tenure, and we hope they found their JAPA experience rewarding.

In 2009 JAPA instituted a process of staggered terms for Associate Editors. I want to thank Leon Hoffman and Lawrence Friedman, whose terms ended in 2009, for their hard work and thoughtful insights over the past many years. Also, Robert S. White will be leaving JAPA as internet editor. Bob did a yeomanly job of handling the JAPA Netcast, and we are very grateful for the time he was able to dedicate to the journal.

We welcome the following new Editorial Board members, whose terms officially began on January 1: Stephen B. Bernstein, Daria Colombo, Anne Erreich, James P. Frosch, James F. Leckman, Janice Lieberman, Ellen Pinsky, Donnel Stern, and Jeanine M. Vivona. In addition, we officially welcome four new Editorial Associates, who have been hard at work since last spring: Michael Garfinkle, Elizabeth Rottenberg, Bret R. Rutherford, and Britt-Marie Schiller.

Also in 2009, to encourage new authors to submit their work to a major journal, JAPA initiated a First Paper Prize. We are pleased to have a submission that may be a candidate for this award in 2010. Details about the First Paper Prize appear in this issue, and we encourage readers to assist new authors in bringing fresh ideas to our ever changing field.

Finally, as you may be aware, the American Psychoanalytic Association will celebrate its one hundredth anniversary in 2011. JAPA

[[RunningHead]]

Theory, Practice, and Story

Dare to Be Human: A Contemporary Psychoanalytic Journey. By Michael Shoshani Rosenbaum. New York: Routledge, Taylor & Francis, 2009, 215 pp., $34.95.

Richard Honig

Richard Honig

3001 Highland Avenue
Cincinnati, OH 45219
E-mail: rhonig@3001.us

Richard Honig  

With Dare to Be Human, Michael Shoshani Rosenbaum presents an astonishingly open and accessible account of a psychoanalytic treatment. In undertaking its writing he seeks to redress “the profound lack of case studies describing the development of the therapeutic or analytic process from beginning to end” (p. xxiv) and the damage psychoanalysis has caused itself through mystification of the treatment process. He also wishes to redress the dearth of case studies understandable to the nonanalyst. In addition, the book may be unique in its inclusion of commentary on the therapeutic experience written by the patient himself. Rosenbaum explains at the outset his intent to interweave three narrative strands: the account of his patient Daniel's treatment, his perspective on his growth as a therapist (Daniel was one of his first analytic patients), and his views on the impact of the relational perspective on contemporary psychoanalysis.

All this leads to the book's potential to attract a broad audience. The case history, written in a style as akin to storytelling as it is to clinical case reporting, will be accessible to a lay audience and perhaps of relevance to people considering analytic therapy and wishing an introduction to the treatment method and its potential for transformative life change. For the contemporary student of psychoanalysis, the book offers exposure to the major challenges and dilemmas with which the analyst must wrestle in both the personal and interpersonal realms. For the seasoned analyst, Rosenbaum confronts the complex issues facing contemporary psychoanalysis in an era of theoretical pluralism and discusses them in a manner that invites creative dialogue rather than ideological warfare.

The book also distinguishes itself as an unusually personal account of a treatment process from the perspective of the analyst. Rosenbaum's own history resonates powerfully with that of Daniel. Both of them suffered profound psychic wounds at the hands of parents who beat them.

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert Rose

25 Sammis Street
P.O. Box 215
Rowayton, CT 06853
E-mail: giljrose@optonline.net

Gilbert Rose  

This slim book can be read as a moving elegy to a life of achievement and analytic search for the solace of meaning—especially while approaching, in the words of Robert Browning, “the last of life, for which the first was made.”

596

A foreword by Oliver Sacks neatly summarizes the physical basis for the music experienced involuntarily by Leo Rangell in the years following his second cardiac bypass operation in 1995. Benign auditory hallucinations, commonly associated with impaired vision or hearing, stem from abnormal activity in the brain related to the loss of sensory input. These “release phenomena” reflect a hyperactive state of brain networks emitting random bubbles—in this case, music.

Before the operation, Rangell had been hard of hearing. The added stimulus of diminished blood supply to his brain during the operation pushed his auditory system past some threshold, stimulating it to generate its own musical sounds.

When he began writing this book in 2005, Rangell was ninety-two years old. In 1980 he had had successful open-heart bypass surgery for five obstructed coronary vessels. The result was excellent; he was playing tennis again in a little over two weeks. For years he lived asymptomatically on collateral medication, but by 1995 the same vessels were again almost completely obstructed. He had the second operation and after a few days in which he experienced periods of confusion was ready to leave the hospital within a week. A wire was left in his chest, a reminder that a pacemaker might be necessary in the future.

As early as the first few days in the ICU, he became aware of hearing singing outside his hospital room, even late into the night. The songs and music were still present when he returned safely to his home. When his wife and his son and daughter corrected his perception, it dawned on him: “My God, it's me. They are coming from me. They are in me somewhere. I am singing” (p. 17).

The song, tune, or rhythm would be the first signal of a prevailing mood, whether happy or sad. There was no indication of whether it was the cause or result of some affect, idea, or combination of the two. The tunes would cover the whole range of ditties, marches, and old sentimental and romantic favorites—all in a personal biographical context. Like any mental product, they would designate the nature of his psychological state at that particular moment. In the course of time he learned to “decathect” them for the most part.

Perspectives

The Untold War: Inside the Hearts, Minds, and Souls of Our Soldiers. By Nancy Sherman. New York: W. W. Norton, 2010, 338 pp., $27.95.

Prudence Gourguechon

Prudence Gourguechon

1454 West Farragut Avenue
Chicago, IL 60640
E-mail: prudygourguechon@gmail.com

Prudence Gourguechon  

With characteristic directness and clarity, the philosopher and psychoanalytic scholar Nancy Sherman puts the core argument of her poignant and challenging book, The Untold War, right up front on page one: “That psychological anguish in war is also moral anguish is a fact too often ignored. Studies of war trauma tend to focus on the acute psychological hardships of crossing the borders of peace and war. But the research … misses the ubiquity of the inner war and its subtle moral contours.” Sherman argues compellingly that “as a public we, too, need to know how war feels [morally, to those who fight it], for war's residue should not just be a soldier's private burden. It ought to be something that we, who do not don the uniform, recognize and understand as well” (p. 8). Her argument echoes Robert Jay Lifton's efforts a generation ago to bring the guilt of war into the commons (1976). The public and the military community need to accept as a fact the moral anguish soldiers experience.

Sherman's thesis illuminates a valuable lesson for psychoanalysts. It seems we often ignore the dimension of moral injury and conflict. Freud of course laid out the structural terrain of moral conflict, with his elaboration of the superego and ego ideal and the forces that may be at war with them. But the specific content and nuances of moral conflicts themselves, and the particulars of the suffering experienced when they become intense or unmanageable, are often overlooked in clinical considerations.

In the arena of the suffering endured by American soldiers fighting in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries, this moral dimension has been underrepresented in both psychoanalytic and other mental health perspectives. For example, a key study of the mental health consequences of the Iraq and Afghanistan wars, issued by the Rand Corporation (Tanielien and Jaycox 2008), describes the core injuries of soldiers as PTSD (an anxiety disorder), depression, and traumatic brain injury.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“This Strange Disease”: Adolescent Transference and the Analyst's Sexual Orientation

John Burton and Karen Gilmore

Karen Gilmore

Suite 10A
101 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: johnkburtonmd@gmail.com

320 East 18th Street
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: kjg23@columbia.edu

Karen Gilmore   Clinical Professor, Division of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry, Columbia College of Physicians and Surgeons; Senior Associate Director, Director of the Child Division, and Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

The treatment of adolescents by gay analysts is uncharted territory regarding the impact of the analyst's sexuality on the analytic process. Since a core challenge of adolescence involves the integration of the adult sexual body, gender role, and reproductive capacities into evolving identity, and since adolescents seek objects in their environment to facilitate both identity formation and the establishment of autonomy from primary objects, the analyst's sexual orientation is arguably a potent influence on the outcome of adolescent development. However, because sexual orientation is a less visible characteristic of the analyst than gender, race, or age, for example, the line between reality and fantasy is less clearly demarcated. This brings up special considerations regarding discovery and disclosure in the treatment. To explore these issues, the case of a late adolescent girl in treatment with a gay male analyst is presented. In this treatment, the question of the analyst's sexual orientation, and the demand by the patient for the analyst's self-disclosure, became a transference nucleus around which the patient's individual dynamics and adolescent dilemmas could be explored and clarified.

Psychoanalytic and Musical Ambiguity: The Tritone in Gee, Officer Krupke

Julie Nagel

Julie Nagel

400 Maynard Street
Suite 706
Ann Arbor, Ml 48104
E-mail: jjnagel@comcast.net

Julie Nagel   Faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Co-Chair, Committee on Psychoanalysis and the Arts, American Psychoanalytic Association.

The poignant and timeless Broadway musical West Side Story is viewed from the standpoint of taking musical forms as psychoanalytic data. The musical configuration of notes called the tritone (or diabolus in musica) is taken as a sonic metaphor expressing ambiguity both in musical vocabulary and in mental life. The tritone, which historically and harmonically represents instability, is heard throughout the score and emphasizes the intrapsychic, interpersonal, and social dramas that unfold within and between the two gangs in West Side Story. Particular emphasis is given to the comic but exceedingly sober song Gee, Officer Krupke. Bernstein's sensitivity to the ambiguity and tension inherent in the tritone in West Side Story is conceptualized as an intersection of music theory and theories of mind; this perspective holds implications for clinical practice and transports psychoanalytic concepts from the couch to the Broadway stage and into the community to address the complexities of love, hate, aggression, prejudice, and violence. Ultimately, West Side Story cross-pollinates music and theater, as well as music and psychoanalytic concepts.

We never got the love that ev'ry child ought-a get

—West Side Story vocal score, p. 173

There's a time for us, Some day a time for us…. We'll find a new way of living, … We'll find a way of for-giving … Some-how, Some day,… Some-where!

—West Side Story vocal score, pp. 163-165

The first Century of Psychoanalytic Ideas: Toward Greater Scientific Empiricism

One Hundred Years of Psychoanalysis, a Timeline: 1900-2000. By Elisabeth Young-Bruehl and Christine Dunbar. Designed by Isabelle Roussel. Toronto: Caversham Productions, www.cavershamproductions.com, 2009, 24 pp., $29.95 (Canadian).

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065
E-mail: hoffman.leon@gmail.com

Leon Hoffman  

“The medium is the message is a phrase coined by Marshall McLuhan meaning that the form of a medium embeds itself in the message, creating a symbiotic relationship by which the medium influences how the message is perceived. The phrase was introduced in his most widely known book, Understanding Media: The Extensions of Man, published in 1964. McLuhan proposes that media itself, not the content it carries, should be the focus of study. He said that a medium affects the society in which it plays a role not only by the content delivered over the medium, but also by the characteristics of the medium itself” (Wikipedia 2009).

In One Hundred Years of Psychoanalysis, a Timeline: 1900-2000 by Elisabeth Young-Bruehl and Christine Dunbar and designed by Isabelle Roussel we are presented an attractive, colorful, two-meter-long folded document with print on both sides of ten panels—a medium quite different from the materials traditionally used in the education of psychoanalysts.

As clinical psychoanalysts we privilege the spoken word, which is to be listened to and responded to with words—a live conversation, regardless how unequal the contributions to the conversation may be. One participant in the dyad speaks more, and the other tries to listen as carefully as he or she can. This very private interpersonal interaction occurs in the privacy of a space from which others are excluded. When the two participants are at a distance from one another, the communication may occur over the telephone or, more recently, on the computer (via Skype or a web-cam).

[[RunningHead]]

Toward Developing a Scale to Empirically Measure Psychotic Defense Mechanisms

Stephen Beck

Stephen Beck

7401 Rue Hochalaga
Montreal, Quebec H1N 3M5
Canada
E-mail: prometheas.constantinides@gmail.com

Stephen Beck  

In recent years, research on psychological processes has greatly advanced our understanding of various psychopathologies. Defense mechanisms, for example, have been described as observable phenomena, and studies have shown meaningful relationships among these processes and other measurable dimensions of health and illness. Despite some notable exceptions, one area that has been overlooked is the empirical study of defenses in psychotic disorders. This is in part due to the lack of valid instruments measuring psychotic-level defenses. Propadeutic to creating an empirical scale for measuring psychotic defenses, the psychoanalytic and empirical literature on psychotic defenses is reviewed, after which the concept of psychotic defenses as measured by the P-DMRS (Psychotic Defense Mechanism Rating Scales)—which can be used independently or in combination with the current DMRS (Defense Mechanism Rating Scales)—is operationalized. Finally, current research directions applying the P-DMRS to the clinical setting are presented, as well as its implications for guiding current clinical practice.

J.K. Shedler (2010). The efficacy of psychodynamic psychotherapy.American Psychologist65:98-109.

Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford  

The belief that “there is no research” on psychoanalysis or psychodynamic psychotherapy continues to permeate some quarters of psychiatry and psychology, which is surprising and even distressing to many investigators actively conducting clinical research. For some, a bias against the recognition of psychoanalytic research may be an understandable reaction to the past excesses of psychoanalytic authority, but it is curious that even some psychoanalysts do not seem aware of or interested in ongoing research in their field. Starting from these observations, Jonathan Shedler recently published a review article on psychodynamic psychotherapy research that lays out the distinctive features of psychodynamic psychotherapy, evaluates evidence for its efficacy, and raises a number of interesting issues in comparative psychotherapy research.

[[RunningHead]]

Is Infant Research Useful in Clinical Work with Adults?

Sarah Ackerman

Sarah Ackerman

53 South Main Street, Suite 311
Hanover, NH 03755
E-mail: ackerman@post.harvard.edu

Sarah Ackerman  

At a conference in London in 2000, André Green and Daniel Stern engaged in a daylong metatheoretical debate about the role or relevance of research-based models of the infant mind and its development in the clinical work of psychoanalysis. Stern advocated the utility of infant research, highlighting, for example, the way research on the relatedness of newborns challenges Freud's conceptualization of primary narcissism. Green countered that “the reality of intrapsychic life makes any form of observational study impossible or at least impossibly naive” (Denman 2001, p. 550). By employing Freud's concept of Nachträglichkeit, deferred action, he argued, “any notion of the ‘now’ as relevant to psychoanalysis is instantly destroyed, corroded if you like, by our post hoc reconstructions” (p. 550).

The panel “Is Infant Research Useful in Clinical Work with Adults?” departed from this highly theoretical debate to home in on clinical concerns. Chair Anne Erreich focused on “whether infant research provides novel conceptualizations for the relationship between two minds in the motherinfant dyad which may then be reenacted in the transference relationships.” The concern here was a pragmatic one: does infant research further our understanding of transference in any meaningful way? In addressing this question, Erreich invited two speakers to represent contrasting positions on the subject: Nasir Ilahi, an analyst with a British object relations and post-Kleinian perspective, and Doris Silverman, an analyst who relies on systematic infant research, when it is appropriately available, to inform her work. These two speakers presented their theoretical positions, using clinical case material to illustrate their approach. The Italian analyst Jorge Canestri, European editor of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis and chair of the Working Party on Theoretical Issues of the European Psychoanalytic Federation, offered a discussion that drew out several points warranting further consideration.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interpretation of Action in Psychoanalysis

Dominique Scarfone

Dominique Scarfone

825 Avenue Dunlop
Montréal, QC H2V 2W6
CANADA
E-mail: dominique.scarfone@umontreal.ca

Dominique Scarfone  

What psychoanalysts most apparently do is provide a locus for the spoken word, a place to recover the ancient magic of words, as Freud (1890) would have it. In our present times, where hyperactive multitaskers are the proposed models for imitation, our consulting rooms are perhaps the havens where it is still possible to pause and let the words come as if of their own will. Listening to one's own inner discourse is made easier for the analysand thanks to the listening offered by the analyst. The analysand's universe can therefore, at some point, seem to be lined by a broad if frail layer of words. Interlaced with one another, words then appear as if working their way toward covering the whole scene. Suddenly, however, the scene itself is on fire! Action bursts in, unpredictable and apparently unmanageable through the words that had seemed so useful and dominant just a few moments before. A salutary reminder that, for all their usefulness, words are effective in analysis possibly through borrowing their strength from the action they represent and which, in spite of appearances, had never been completely transmuted into representation. Words, after all, are a form of action.

To a naive spectator, psychoanalytic conversation around this sort of problem may at times seem to take a theological slant. In view of the preceding remarks, for instance, do we rather adhere to John's gospel which, in its opening, states that “in the beginning was the Word,” or to the Faustian verse cited by Freud in closing Totem and Taboo—“In the beginning there was action”? The question is anything but theoretical, since it concerns the practice of psychoanalysis in its most significant aspects, sometimes confronting the analyst with at once urgent and difficult choices. The international panel held at the 2009 Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association addressed precisely that sort of problem.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 17, 2009. Panelists: Henry Smith (chair), Paul Denis, Jay Greenberg, John Steiner.

[[RunningHead]]

Vulnerability to Psychosis: A Psychoanalytic Study of the Nature and Therapy of the Psychotic State. By Franco De Masi. Translated by Philip Slotkin et al. London: Karnac Books, 2009, 312 pp., $39.95.

Gregorio Hautmann

Gregorio Hautmann

Via Cittadella 39
Florence
ITALY
E-mail: gregorio.hautmann@gmail.com

Gregorio Hautmann  

Franco De Masi's Vulnerability to Psychosis is a successful attempt to illustrate by clinical examples a theoretical psychoanalytic model of psychosis which, while personal, belongs firmly within a tradition whose

599

principal forebears are Bion, Meltzer, Rosenfeld, and the post-Kleinians in general—a tradition that is further enriched by the contributions of other authors, among whom Winnicott, Fonagy, and Aulagnier stand out.

The book presents the experience of De Masi as an analyst at work with his patients or in supervisions, revealing not only a personal style but also a range of theoretical and technical instruments of his own. An atmosphere of great sincerity emerges from his descriptions of difficult phases of analyses, with their moments of incomprehension, feelings of inadequacy, and therapeutic disappointment.

The image conveyed is that of an expert analyst supported by a solid theoretical and technical frame of reference, who is, however, also cautious and imbued with anything but a sense of therapeutic omnipotence, and who seems to talk a lot to his patients; one's abiding impression is of an analyst who is at all times curious and intent on understanding—an analyst who remains stubbornly convinced that understanding will be the way to reach and help the patient.

I will try to outline some of the salient aspects of De Masi's model, while confining any comments, a large number of which might be suggested by a reading of this book, to a minimum.

In developing his argument, the author considers anew the concept of the unconscious (chapter 4 reproduces what is now surely a celebrated contribution originally published in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis in 2000). Taking as his starting point the complexity already presented by this concept in Freud, as well as the shifts of meaning to which it has been subject in the various psychoanalytic schools and the work of various authors—the same could, as it happens, be said of many other terms used in psychoanalysis—De Masi finds it helpful to distinguish at least two senses of the word unconscious, which probably correspond to different functions of the mind: the dynamic unconscious and the emotional unconscious. The first is the repressed unconscious discovered by Freud, knowledge of which was developed and amplified by Melanie Klein with her conceptualization of unconscious fantasy and the prominence she gave to the mechanisms of splitting and projection, culminating in the notion of projective identification. The second is the unconscious described by Bion, who stresses its function not as a repository for the repressed, but as a metabolizer of psychic experiences, which must be in proper working order if the mind is to be able to produce thoughts.

Psychoanalytic Theories

Trauma and Human Existence: Autobiographical, Psychoanalytic, and Philosophical Reflections. By Robert D. Stolorow. New York: The Analytic Press, 2007, 62 pp., $19.95.

Dori Laub

Dori Laub

30 Ranch Road
Woodbridge, CT 06525
E-mail: Laub37@hotmail.com

Dori Laub  

This book places affect—that is, subjective emotional experience (in contradistinction to drive)—at the center and as the framework of psychological life. This “entails a radical contextualization of virtually all aspects of human life” (p. 1) within a dynamic intersubjective field. Affect regulation makes possible one's ability to tolerate and experience one's feeling, and the process of emotional attunement is necessary for affect regulation to properly develop. When such attunement is absent, and “severe emotional pain finds no validating relational home in which it can be held” (p. 10), it becomes an unendurable, unintegratable state that constitutes emotional trauma. In this book Robert Stolorow draws on philosophical reflections, primarily those of Heidegger, to further elucidate the phenomenology of emotional trauma, lending it depth and richness, and contextualizing it in the ontology of the human condition.

The book proceeds simultaneously along three interwoven lines: the psychoanalytic, the autobiographical, and the philosophical. The first line, the psychoanalytic, charts a theory of intersubjectivity: the idea that from birth onward affect is regulated, or dysregulated, within ongoing relational systems. Thus, emotional attunement is crucial for a child's development of somatic-linguistic integration, which can be maintained or compromised at any time of life. The author then turns to the elucidation of an intersubjective theory of developmental trauma. This type of trauma occurs when there is a malattunement to painful affect, “a breakdown of the child-caregiver system of mutual regulation” (p. 3) that leads to unintegrated affective states and lifelong emotional conflict and vulnerability.

Following on his initial insights into the consequences of early relational trauma, Stolorow presents a more composite theory, one involving both mental agencies and dynamic functioning, that extends into the realm of clinical psychoanalysis.

Melodies in My Mind: The Polyphony of Mental Life

Julie Nagel

Julie Nagel

400 Maynard Street
Suite 706
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: jjnagel@comcast.net

Julie Nagel   Faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Co-Chair, Committee on Psychoanalysis and the Arts, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Delivering the Gertrude and Ernst Ticho Memorial Lecture today is a deeply personal honor and a professional milestone. To be respected by colleagues, appreciated by friends, and loved by one's family is sufficient reward for anyone. Today I have the additional privilege of sharing with you my thoughts about music and psychoanalysis. The Ticho Lectureship acknowledges early- to mid-career psychoanalysts; this recognition raises an interesting paradox. Implied in the designation “early- to mid-career” is the notion that a person younger than myself would receive this award. In the context of age and career, when I talk with analytic colleagues, of all ages and from various cities (and also when reading data in the report on APsaA's 2006 Survey of Analytic Practice [Brauer, Brauer, and Falk 2008] before the recent recession), questions are raised about the average age of analysts, attracting candidates, unwelcome open hours in analytic schedules, and compromised incomes. My musician colleagues (supported by recent data from the National Endowment for the Arts) express concern about “graying audiences” and the future of classical music. (For clarification, I use the term classical music in this paper to refer to tonal, harmonic music in the Western compositional tradition. West Side Story, the focus of my musical example later in my lecture, is steeped in that tradition.) Qualms about the future of psychoanalysis and the diminishing concert-attending public are not based in illusion; both concerns reflect realities and anxieties but also challenges and opportunities, regardless of birthdates and stages in a career. While there are nonclassical musics and various approaches to psychoanalytic practice, the questions remain:

Gertrude and Ernst Ticho Memorial Lecture, American Psychoanalytic Association, Washington, DC, June 11, 2010. The author thanks Linda Brakel, Charles Burch, and Louis Nagel, who made helpful comments on earlier drafts, Warren Procci, who chaired the lecture, and Glen Gabbard, who introduced her the night of the lecture and, more than thirty years ago, showed her a way of thinking that contributed to her becoming a psychoanalyst. Submitted for publication July 5, 2010.

Anne Ward
24 Ashleigh Road
London SE20 7YW
United Kingdom
E-mail: aimward@freeuk.corn

Christine Miqueu-Baz   Private practice of psychoanalysis, London.

There is widespread concern regarding various aspects of psychoanalytic education. A recent paper by Patrick Casement, drawn widely from his long experience as a supervisor both in the U.K. and abroad, implied that the British Psychoanalytical Society is not exempt from these concerns. To investigate this, a semistructured, anonymous questionnaire was devised and sent to all candidates and all recently qualified analysts at the society. Overall there was a 58% response rate, with 77% of candidates and 39% of recently qualified analysts responding. Concerns were expressed about aspects of the training, but on the whole these were balanced by appreciation. Although strong criticism was expressed by a minority, it seems that when something goes wrong for a candidate, this experience is felt keenly by the peer group as well. The results are discussed in the context of the current training and ethos of the British Society, as well as in relation to a more widespread move toward “competency-based” education. The maturational tasks facing both candidates and trainers are also addressed.

Psyche and Soma

La Petite Maison De L'Âme. By Laurence Kahn. Paris: Èditions Gallimard, 1993, 284 pp., €17.00.

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: lewi_Kirshner@hms.harvard.edu

Lewis Kirshner  

Laurence Kahn is a psychoanalyst member of the Association Française de Psychanalyse, a classical scholar and linguist with expertise in Greek philosophy and German romanticism, and a child psychotherapist. Her work came to wider attention with her masterful report on “la figurabilité” (figuration) at the Sixty-first Congress of French-speaking psychoanalysts in 2001, which revealed a powerful analytic mind and an extensive knowledge of Freud's texts. La Petite Maison de L'âme (The Little House of the Soul) is a collection of essays that together and separately take up a number of unresolved issues in psychoanalytic theory and practice, including Freud's conceptions of history and the infantile, and, pari passu, in Greek mythology, as if to demonstrate a kind of latent unity of thought that points to a fundamental truth of origins. It is a dense and difficult work that distills many of the central preoccupations of French psychoanalysis, of which it must be considered one of the jewels in the crown. I mean this in two senses: it is at once a brilliant achievement of integration and scholarship and a kind of museum piece, a highlight of a remarkable period of psychoanalytic creativity in the last half of the twentieth century.

In a conference of the Montreal Psychoanalytic Society in 2005, Kahn expressed concern about the future of Freudian psychoanalysis, which seems to be veering away from its classic focus on infantile sexuality as the substratum of neurosis and the motor force behind the transference. In this regard, Kahn has a penchant for unraveling contradictions and paradoxes in Freud's thinking that touch on the major issues psychoanalysis will have to confront in order to survive as a relevant theoretical enterprise, rather than being relegated to the status of a fascinating interlude of intellectual history. Her careful and admiring reading of Freud, more typical of French analytic writing than North American, oscillates between a homage to the master and a challenge to the difficulties of his theory. This duality begins with the first essay, “The Fixed and the Migratory” (my translation)

Experience of Learning

Learning from Life: Becoming a Psychoanalyst. By Patrick Casement. New York: Routledge, 2006, 202 pp., $35.00.

Sybil Houlding

Sybil Houlding

129 Church Street, Suite 308
New Haven, CT 06510
E-mail: s.houlding@comcast.net

Sybil Houlding  

In the introduction to the first volume of his “Learning from…” quartet—Learning from the Patient—Patrick Casement (1985) quotes Bion: “the psycho-analyst needs to be able to question himself as often as, and as long as, he is unsatisfied, but should not spend too much time looking for the answer in books. The time we have is limited, so we must read people” (Bion 1974, p. 17).

This quote nicely sums up Casement's attitude toward the canon. Like Bion, Casement is not interested in received wisdom. Unless he can make an idea his own, it is not useful to him. In Learning from Life he reveals that for many years he could not accept the idea of projective identification, until one day he had an extremely powerful experience that brought the concept to life. A training and supervising analyst at the British Psychoanalytical Society, he is steeped in the post-Kleinian literature yet wears his learning lightly; as the affinity with Bion suggests, there is a tension here, as if acknowledging the work of others would distract him from his own experience.

Casement introduced the concept of the internal supervisor in his first volume, along with trial identification, and these are the ideas for which he is best known. Above all, however, Casement is known as a generous clinician who writes in a lively and immediate way about clinical work, bringing in the theory behind his case examples rather quietly; he is often cited as a good author for teaching beginning therapists, whether social caseworkers or analysts, and this is his great strength.

His “Some Pressures on the Analyst for Physical Contact during the Re-living of an Early Trauma,” first published in 1982, illuminates issues very much at the heart of current psychoanalytic discourse. Casement pinpoints the tension between theorists who wish to offer a new, perhaps “corrective” experience with a new “better” object and those who view this as an avoidance of pain in the current analytic encounter.

Jack Novick

Jack Novick

617 Stratford Drive
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: jnovick@umich.edu

Jack Novick  

This is one in a new series of psychoanalytic books (Contemporary Psychoanalytic Studies) with Jon Mills as editor and a prestigious and quite representative group of analysts serving on the advisory board. Their choice to include a book titled Endings speaks to the current moment in the avoidance/engagement cycle regarding termination. For the first fifty years of the twentieth century, psychoanalysts had little to say on the topic and were rather cavalier in the way they dealt with termination. But after a series of symposia by British, American, and French analysts, termination became a topic of interest and investigation, especially in the United States. Articles, reports, and books on termination appeared with increasing frequency. Engagement with the topic seemed to reach a peak in the 1990s. At that point there was general agreement that there is a distinct phase of treatment that can be designated the termination phase.

B.M. Rottman, W. Ahn, C.A. Sanislow, & N.S. Kim (2009). Can clinicians recognize DSM-IV personality disorders from five-factor model descriptions of patient cases?American Journal of Psychiatry166:427-433.

J. Ablon

J. Ablon

J. Ablon  

Historically, the DSM system of diagnostic classification has served to widen the gulf between a depth psychology understanding of psychopathology and a more medically oriented model. A welcome change may be on the horizon, as significantly reworking the current system for diagnosing personality disorders has been proposed for the next revision of the DSM. One suggestion has been to dimensionalize personality assessment using either a five-factor model (FFM) or a measure like the Shedler-Westen Assessment Procedure (SWAP). For most psychoanalytic clinicians this would represent a step in the right direction—though by no means the answer to a more complex and individualized approach to understanding psychiatric illness.

In this article, Rottman and colleagues begin by reviewing the empirical literature comparing FFM and current DSM-IV systems for diagnosing personality disorders. The results of previous research raise concerns whether the FFM descriptions are too ambiguous and without context.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“You, I, We Created the Poet”: Anne Sexton's Recorded Therapy, November 1963

Dawn Skorczewski

Dawn Skorczewski

200 Main Street
Waltham, MA 02149
E-mail: dawnskor@brandeis.edu

Dawn Skorczewski   Associate Professor of English and Director of University Writing, Brandeis University.

In 1991, when it was revealed that the psychiatrist Martin Orne had released tapes of his therapy sessions with Anne Sexton to her biographer, mental health professionals expressed concern and outrage. Those who actually listen to the controversial tapes would be curious to find a debate between Sexton and Orne about mental illness, creativity, and therapeutic process. To what extent did Sexton's creative accomplishments point to aspects of her psychiatric progress that might otherwise have been overlooked? While Sexton asserted her achievements and sought affirmation from her psychiatrist, Orne persistently responded by stating that the poetry is not as important as the person. Their different ways of understanding the relationships between poetry and therapeutic process speak volumes about the power of the creative imagination to challenge existing structures of thought, even structures designed to define the psyche itself.

[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Addiction as Self-Medication: Finding Hope behind the Pain. By Edward J. Khantzian and Mark J. Albanese. New York: Rowman & Littlefield, 2008, xvii + 157 pp., $42.95.

William Gottdiener

William Gottdiener

445 West 59th Street
New York, NY 10019
E-mail: wgottdiener@jjay.cuny.edu

William Gottdiener  

The field of psychoanalysis has had a long-standing and conflicted relationship with the problem of addictive disorders (Gottdiener 2008; Levin and Weiss 1994). Many psychoanalytic writers have discussed ways in

1028

which psychoanalytic theory has brought understanding to this problem, and some writers have discussed ways in which psychoanalysis, conducted with or without parameters (Eissler 1954) or modifications (Rothstein 1998), has been successfully applied clinically (see, e.g., Dodes 1996; Dowling 1995; Johnson 1999, 2001; Levin and Weiss 1994; Petrucelli and Stuart 2001; Shaffer and Burglass 1981; Weegman and Cohen 2002; Yalisove 1997). Even so, there have always been members of the psychoanalytic community who think that psychoanalysis has little to offer toward understanding or treating people with addictive disorders (Levin and Weiss 1994).

Despite the conflicts that psychoanalysis has had with the problem of addictions, the self-medication hypothesis—a psychoanalytic theory of addictive disorders developed by Edward Khantzian in 1985—has had considerable impact on the fields of psychoanalysis and addictionology. Since the emergence of the self-medication hypothesis, the idea that people abuse drugs, gamble excessively, and so on in order to self-medicate a personal problem has become commonplace; indeed the term self-medicating is often used by patients in treatment. The self-medication hypothesis is even widely cited and used—with alterations—by clinicians and theoreticians working from the cognitive-behavioral perspective (see Blume, Schmaling, and Marlatt 2000).

In one way, the self-medication hypothesis is similar to other psychoanalytic models of addictive disorders, in that it states that most people who develop an addictive disorder do so because they are using it to reduce or eliminate unpleasure. Smoking a joint, chugging whiskey, or repeatedly pulling the lever of a slot machine are ways to gain some measure of control over what we feel.

Compared with other psychoanalytically oriented models of addictive disorders, however, the self-medication hypothesis argues that a person's addictive behavior, including a person's drug of choice, does not occur randomly. A chosen addictive behavior is serendipitously discovered to be the best way of helping a person cope with his or her specific experience of unpleasure—heroin's narcotic effect puts to sleep the rage that heroin users frequently feel.

[[RunningHead]]

The Effect of Attachment-Related Stress on the Capacity to Mentalize: An Fmri Investigation of the Biobehavioral Switch Model

Tobias Nolte , Caitlin Hudac , Linda Mayes , Peter Fonagy , Sidney Blatt and Kevin Pelphrey

Kevin Pelphrey

E-mail: tobias.nolte@annafreud.org

Kevin Pelphrey  

Fonagy and colleagues have conceptualized a decoupling effect of the activated attachment system on the capacity to mentalize (Fonagy and Target 2008). Neuroimaging studies provide evidence for this mechanism but drew on study designs that did not specifically operationalize the relationship between attachment activation and broader social cognition (Bartels and Zeki 2004). Bowlby's original notion of internal working models of attachment that come on line when a person is exposed to attachment-related stress (e.g., death, loss, other relational trauma) has as yet not been subjected to rigorous neuroscientific investigation. Behaviorally, however, it is known that secondary strategies come into play when direct security seeking, due to an inaccessibility of the attachment figure or its psychic representation, fails. The brain mechanisms of these processes of hyper-activation (perceptual vigilance) and deactivation (perceptual defense), as attempts to adapt to a suboptimal environment, have not yet been explored (Mikulincer and Shaver 2007).
