responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ethics Education in Psychoanalytic Training: A Survey

Paul Ransohoff

Paul Ransohoff

885 Oak Grove Avenue, Suite 208
Menlo Park, CA 94025
E-mail: paul.ransohoff@gte.net

Paul Ransohoff   Faculty and Co-Chair of the Ethics and Impairment Committee, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

Didactic education in psychoanalytic ethics is a relatively new phenomenon. Ethics courses were offered by few institutes before they were mandated and before publication of the first Ethics Case Book in 2001. As institutes have developed ethics training, the solutions they have arrived at—formats, length and placement of courses, and preferred readings—remain unknown to other educators and analysts. This survey was undertaken to gain an overview of the current state of ethics education. Twenty-nine of the thirty-one training institutes of the American Psychoanalytic Association (93%) responded to inquiries. Most institutes (79%) offered one course, and the average number of class sessions was 6.3. Of 258 different readings used, 61 (23.6%) were used by more than one institute and 37 (14.3%) by more than two. The most frequent topics were boundaries, confidentiality, and illness, and Dewald and Clark's Case Book (2008) and Gabbard and Lester (1995) were the most common readings. These findings should be useful to instructors, curriculum committees, and ethics committees in their ethics education planning, as well as to practicing analysts in their ethical self-education. This study may also serve as a model for analogous investigations into other areas of analytic education and as an impetus to further research and educational innovation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Plying the Steel: A Reconsideration of Surgical Metaphors in Psychoanalysis

Gavin Ivey

Gavin Ivey

Private Bag 3 Wits 2050
Johannesburg, Gauteng
SOUTH AFRICA
E-mail: gavin.ivey@wits.ac.za

Gavin Ivey   Coordinator, Ph.D, psychology program, University of the Witwaterstand, Johannesberg; Senior Lecturer in Psychology, Victoria University, Melbourne; Editor, Psycho-Analytic Psychotherapy in South Africa. Submitted for publication July 26, 2009.

Among the metaphors that Freud used to describe psychoanalysis, the surgical is possibly the most deplored. It is considered an anachronistic remnant of a dubious medical ideology that psychoanalysis has largely renounced. However, while analysts today avoid surgical analogies, their patients continue to produce surgical fantasies about analytic treatment. This fact alone requires a serious consideration of the meanings that surgical metaphors have for them. A second reason for reconsidering the role of the surgical metaphor, from the analyst's perspective, lies in its creative revival by W. R. Bion. Disregarding the shift away from surgical analogizing, Bion employed the metaphor to vividly portray various aspects of the analytic situation and the patient's experience of them. A brief historical overview of the surgical metaphor in psychoanalysis is provided, followed by an account of the reasons for its demise and by a review of the criticisms that continue to be leveled at it. Bion's use of surgical metaphors toward the end of his life is then explored. Finally, illustrations are given of the various ways in which patients use spontaneous surgical metaphors to depict transference and the analytic process. Though the analyst should not deliberately adopt surgical metaphors, it is important to remain open to these transference portrayals.

The wounded surgeon plies the steel

That questions the distempered part;

Beneath the bleeding hands we feel

The sharp compassion of the healer's art.

—T. S. Eliot, East Coker1

[[RunningHead]]

The Very Thought of Education: Psychoanalysis and the Impossible Professions. By Deborah P. Britzman. Albany: State University of New York Press, 2009, 166 pp., $60.00 hardcover, $23.95 paperback.

Dawn Skorczewski

Dawn Skorczewski

200 Main Street
Waltham, MA 02149
E-mail: dawnskor@brandeis.edu

Dawn Skorczewski  

For over a decade, Deborah P. Britzman, Distinguished Research Professor of Education at York University, Toronto, has been writing about psychoanalysis and education from the perspective of an award-winning teacher, a teacher of teachers, and an educational researcher. In this most recent work, she adds yet another subject position to the list. She invites us to think about education from the perspective of one who has returned to the classroom at midlife, as a candidate at a psychoanalytic institute. Not surprisingly, analytic training has not changed Britzman's dedication to the exploration of the complex emotional worlds of those involved in education, both teachers and students. As in her previous books, Britzman brings a comprehensive knowledge of psychoanalytic theory, philosophy, and literature to her exploration of ideas about education, helping us to think through some of education's most vexing issues (see Britzman 1991, 1998, 2003a, b, 2006). She seeks to discover “why the idea of education has been so scaled down that it can only refer to what has already happened to us and then what needs to happen to inexperienced others” (p. 7).

Because we develop in school, Britzman contends, our concept of education is imbued with ideas of punishment and reward, and grounded in a concept of knowledge as a transmission of information to be regurgitated. To engage in the psychoanalytic view of education that Britzman imagines, we must turn away from this model and embrace radical uncertainty as the basis of learning. Her sources traverse many disciplines and genres, including novels, art, psychoanalytic theory, clinical material, educational theory, psychology, and philosophy. Reading the models of education represented in these genres. Britzman argues for a psychoanalytic education of the kind with which we are familiar—in an analytic institute—as well as a psychoanalytic understanding of all education, regardless of the institutional context. In any setting, Britzman notes, teachers and students shrink from “the very thought of education” because the word itself triggers “an avalanche of certainty through its measures of success and failure” (p. 2).

Spontaneity: A Psychoanalytic Inquiry. By Gemma Corradi Fiumara. New York: Routledge, 2009, 149 pp., $36.95.

Stephen Leibow

Stephen Leibow

400 Walmer Road, Suite 125
Toronto, Ontario M5P2T9
CANADA
E-mail: srleibow@rogers.com

Stephen Leibow  

Spontaneity: A Psychoanalytic Inquiry, by Gemma Corradi Fiumara, is a welcome book on an intriguing and perplexing subject. The challenge the subject presents begins with the problem of definition. Spontaneity is an ambiguous and multifaceted idea, difficult to describe in clinical terms. Like the idea of the true self or authenticity, it is something we feel we recognize when we see it but have difficulty describing. In her introduction Fiumura takes up the meaning of spontaneity as that which is free and unconstrained, referring to Freud's “personal free will” and “freely aspiring mental activity” (p. 9) and argues with an “imperialistic” notion of science and a determinism that “tells us basically that all of our mental life is to be causally explainable” (p. 6). She places spontaneity on the side of the inner forces, of an “inner constitutive personhood” (p. 8) and the here and now; she is more interested in the “ulterior” perspective, the response to life events, and the “inner actions” linked to “agency, intentionality and initiative” (p. 7). The chief clinical interest appears to be narcissistic disorders and those “devastated patients forever condemning the inadequacy or viciousness of their primordial objects,” who “are actually persons who do not give up analysis and constantly struggle in its pursuit—wilful subjects indeed” (p. 8).

Steven Roose

Steven Roose  

“Psychiatrists have rarely enjoyed a surplus of public trust,” writes Thomas Insel, director of the National Institute of Mental Health, in an editorial in Journal of the American Medical Association, and recent revelations of extensive financial ties between psychiatrists and the pharmaceutical industry have not helped the situation any.

It is now well established that gifts from the pharmaceutical industry, including not only stipends and honoraria but also food and pens, influence the decisions physicians make in treating their patients.

The Work of Confluence: Listening and Interpreting in the Psychoanalytic Field. By Madeleine Baranger and Willy Baranger. Edited by Leticia Glocer Fiorini. London: Karnac Books, 2009, 254 pp., £24.99.

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road
Brookline, MA 02445
E-mail: hbievine@aol.com

Howard Levine  

Willy and Madeleine Baranger were French academics, professors of philosophy and classical literature, respectively, who received their

1231

analytic training in Buenos Aires just after World War II, founded the Uruguayan Psychoanalytic Association in 1955, and became leading figures in Latin American psychoanalysis. Their concept of the psychoanalytic field, which derived from their clinical experience and ideas drawn from the group work of Bion (1961), the phenomenology of Merleau-Ponty, and the Gestalt therapy of Kurt Lewin, anticipated by more than twenty-five years the anglophone North American interest in intersubjectivity, co-construction, and the analytic third. Their collaboration with Jorge Mom produced “The Infantile Psychic Trauma from Us to Freud” (chapter 6), a classic study of Freud's evolving ideas about infantile trauma and the role of posttraumatic experience in the resignification and construction of meaning (Nachträglichkeit or après-coup). While that paper, along with two other classics reprinted in this book, “Process and Non-Process in Analytic Work” (chapter 4, also with Mom as coauthor) and “The Mind of the Analyst: From Listening to Interpretation” (chapter 5, written solely by Madeleine Baranger), have been available to English-speaking readers by virtue of their publication in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, the majority of their work, including the other seven chapters of this book, has not been. It is therefore with great pleasure and expectation that readers may approach this volume.

The starting point of the Barangers' theory is their observation that Freud's recommendations in his papers on technique, while valid some of the time, are not sufficient to deal with the full range of complexities encountered in contemporary psychoanalytic practice. In pursuit of a more comprehensive clinical theory, they approached the analytic situation as an object of study in its own right, independent of the individual dynamics and contributions of patient and analyst. What they concluded was that the patient could no longer be the exclusive and privileged object of study. Once the analytic situation is constituted and the process begun, neither the functioning of the patient nor that of the analyst can be considered independent of the other: rather, “analyst and analysand form a couple that participates in and contributes to a single dynamic process” (Baranger 2005, p. 59). The name they gave to that process—and to the jointly and unconsciously constructed psychic space in which it occurs—is the psychoanalytic field.

A Dictionary of Psychoanalysis

Comprehensive Dictionary of Psychoanalysis. By Salman Akhtar. London: Karnac Books, 2009, xiii + 404 pp., £60.00.

Peter Rudnytsky

Peter Rudnytsky

P.O. Box 117310

Gainesville, FL 32611
E-mail: plr@ufl.edu

Peter Rudnytsky  

The first thing that must be remarked about Salman Akhtar is what he himself has not hesitated to call his “demonic” productivity. A consultation of the Library of Congress catalogue discloses that, in addition to the book under review, he has, in the past three years alone, no fewer than six other authored or edited books to his credit (Akhtar 2008, 2009a, b, 2010; Parens, Blum, and Akhtar 2008; Akhtar and Parens 2009).1 It thus comes as no surprise that what I find most astonishing about the Comprehensive Dictionary of Psychoanalysis is the sheer magnitude of what Akhtar has wrought. With characteristic indefatigability, he surveys in an appendix all the glossaries, dictionaries, and encyclopedias of psychoanalysis— including those devoted solely to titans such as Bion, Lacan, Winnicott, and Klein—that have preceded his own effort. We learn that de Mijolla's International Dictionary (2005), a collective achievement of 463 authors, has a total of 1,563 entries, while Laplanche and Pontalis's classic Language of Psycho-Analysis (1967) contains 327 entries, and the revised edition of Moore and Fine's Psychoanalytic Terms and Concepts (1990) includes 490 entries by 296 contributors. Akhtar does not provide the statistic for his own Comprehensive Dictionary, but in a personal communication he has informed me that the total number of entries is 1,853. Thus, Akhtar has single-handedly surpassed by nearly 300 items the work of the 463 individuals recruited by de Mijolla for the International Dictionary.

1Since submitting this review, I have been made aware of three additional books that apparently escaped the attention of the Library of Congress (O'Neil and Akhtar 2008; Akhtar 2009c, d).1249

Delia Battin

Delia Battin

8 East 96th Street
New Yark, NY 10128

Delia Battin  

Stefano Bolognini's new book, Passaggi Segreti: Teoria e Tecnica della Relazione Interpsichica (Secret Passageways: Theory and Technique of the Interpsychic Relationship), can be seen as a continuation of his Psychoanalytic Empathy (Bolognini 2002). In that book Bolognini showed how the intrapsychic unconscious raw material of psychoanalysis can be arrived at only through interpsychic interventions, empathy being one of the crucial ingredients of that collaborative process. In this new book, it is the entire territory of the unconscious that Bolognini claims can be reached only through an interpsychic approach, one that must trace a great diversity of secret passageways to reach its goal. The elaborate system of passageways that Bolognini conceptualizes is constructed by an analysand-architect who has, however, destroyed all blueprints, making access to these subterranean psychic regions almost impossible. The psychoanalyst must with skill and patience enlist the analysand's curiosity and collaboration as the two travelers embark on this journey into the unknown. The irony, of course, is that what now seems foreign to the analysand was once his native land. Bolognini does not quote T. S. Eliot's “We shall not cease from exploration /And the end of all our exploring / Will be to arrive where we started / And know the place for the first time” (1942, p. 208), but the secret passageways he envisages and helps the “blinded” (in the neurotic sense) analysand reenvis-age and reclaim is a metaphor of exploration not unlike the poet's. Bolognini is not without his own poetic voice as he struggles to word the unconscious that psychopathology has secreted so cleverly and so confoundingly. His fine work on empathy has prepared him no doubt for the equally Herculean task of signposting the uncharted regions of the unconscious.

Bolognini makes it clear that even though the unconscious mind that is being signposted and charted is the unique intrapsychic private property of each individual analysand, it takes an interpsychic dialogue, an interpsychic collaboration, to make any progress. Psychoanalytically, two seems essential for one to be explored thoroughly. A further irony of the metaphor of the two travelers and their agreed-on contract is that theirs is a conflicted collaboration from the get-go.

Varieties of Psychoanalytic Experience

The Red Book: Liber Novus. By C. G. Jung. Edited by Sonu Shamdasani. Translated by Mark Kyburz, John Peck, and Sonu Shamdasani. New York: W. W. Norton, 2009, 416 pp., $195.00.

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065
E-mail: hoffman.leon@gmail.com

Leon Hoffman  

In 1913, C. G. Jung began his “confrontation with the unconscious” via a method he called “active imagination,” by which he translated emotions into images in order “to get to the bottom of his unconscious processes.” This self-exploration lasted until 1930. He first recorded these thoughts and images in black notebooks, which he then revised, recording his reflections in a new book—Liber Novus. Bound in red leather, it included his own paintings and has always been known as the Red Book. Its existence has been known since 1962, but it was published only in 2009.

In 1930 Jung put the Red Book aside; in 1959 he tried to complete it and wrote an epilogue but broke off in mid-sentence for unknown reasons. As late as 1957 (Jung died in 1961 when he almost eighty-six), he said that the Black Books and the Red Book were autobiographical and he did not want them published in his Collected Works. On the frontispiece of this volume is a quote from 1957:

The years, of which I have spoken to you, when I pursued the inner images, were the most important time of my life. Everything else is to be derived from this. It began at that time, and the later details hardly matter anymore. My entire life consisted in elaborating what had burst forth from the unconscious and flooded me like an enigmatic stream and threatened to break me. That was the stuff and material for more than one life. Everything later was merely the outer classification, the scientific elaboration, and the integration into life. But the numinous beginning, which contained everything, was then.

This Red Book

Rita Teusch

Rita Teusch

129 Mount Auburn Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: ritateusch@verizon.net

Rita Teusch  

Ilka Quindeau's Trace and Retranscription presents psychoanalytic scholars and practitioners a riveting discussion on the subject of memory. Both a practicing psychoanalyst and an academic sociologist, Quindeau argues that memory is essentially an intersubjective phenomenon and that a close study of memory reveals how psychic structure is built. Her goal is to offer an intersubjective theory of memory that is based on Freud's theory of memory. Quindeau is convincing in her belief that Freud's theory of memory has too often been erroneously simplified and prematurely discarded. Failure to appreciate the central role of memory in the development of psychic structure, she insists, undermines the explanatory power of psychoanalysis, in both theoretical and clinical dimensions.

responseSet.title: Current Status of the Scientific Study of the Personality Disorders: An Overview of Epidemiological, Longitudinal, Experimental Psychopathology, and Neurobehavioral Perspectives
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Current Status of the Scientific Study of the Personality Disorders: An Overview of Epidemiological, Longitudinal, Experimental Psychopathology, and Neurobehavioral Perspectives

Mark Lenzenweger

Mark Lenzenweger

Binghamton Science IV (G-08)
Binghamton, NY 13902-60000
E-mail: mlenzen@binghamton.edu

Mark Lenzenweger   Distinguished Professor of Psychology, SUNY Binghamton; Adjunct Professor of Psychology, Department of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College.

Research on the nature and development of personality disorders has grown immensely over the past thirty years. A selective summary overview is given of the current status of the scientific study of the personality disorders from several perspectives, including the epidemiological, longitudinal, experimental psychopathology, and neurobehavioral perspectives. From this research, we now know that approximately 10 percent of the general population suffer from a diagnosable personality disorder. Moreover, contrary to nearly a century of theory and clinical pedagogy, modern longitudinal studies clearly suggest that personality disorders decrease in severity over time. The mechanisms by which this change occurs are not understood at present, though it is not likely that change in underlying normal personality systems drives the change in personality disorder. The methods of the experimental psychopathology laboratory, including neuroimaging approaches, are being brought to bear on the nature of personality disorders in efforts to relate neurobiological and neurocognitive functions to personality disorder symptomatology. A model that links personality disorder feature development to underlying, interacting brain-based neurobehavioral systems is reviewed in brief. Current issues and findings illustrative of these developments are given using borderline personality disorder as an exemplar. Finally, areas of intersection between psychoanalytic treatment approaches and the growing science of personality disorder are highlighted.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How do Contemporary Psychoanalysts Treat the Past?

Melvin Bornstein

Melvin Bornstein

625 Purdy Street
Birmingham, MI 48009
E-mail: mborn@med.wayne.edu

Melvin Bornstein  

Joseph Lichtenberg began by explaining the panel's format: the panelists—Glen Gabbard, Darlene Bregman Ehrenberg, and Alexandra Harrison—would each present their theoretical orientation for five minutes, followed by a discussion among the panelists, the chair, and reporter Melvin Bornstein. Then each of the three panelists would present a clinical example for ten minutes, followed by further discussion. Lichtenberg noted that dealing with the past was central to psychoanalysis in many of its early concepts, ranging from the releasing of strangulated affect, to freeing memory from repression, to reconstructing traumatic events. Gradually, with advances in psychoanalytic understanding, the conviction that bringing the past into full consciousness is a major means of obtaining therapeutic effect has faded in the minds of several groups within the analytic world. Many analysts now emphasize working primarily in the present, including the present moment, and conceptualizing the past only as it exists in the present. Other analysts focus on unformulated experience, others on dissociation. A further conceptual shift places the analyst as co-constructor or co-creator of whatever view of the past emerges, rather than as neutral interpreter of the past. Lichtenberg set the panel a number of questions: Is the past to be discovered layer by layer, as in Freud's archaeological metaphor? How much do we picture memories of the past to be subject to drive distortion? How much of the analyst's view of the patient's past is drawn from the analyst's reveries? Is working with model scenes a useful way to explore the past? How does the analyst's own past, sometimes similar, sometimes quite different, play a part in the analytic work with the patient's past?

In describing his theoretical orientation to working with the past, Gabbard stated that he views theory as a helpful metaphor, but limited in its ability to explain and direct.

[[RunningHead]]

My Teaching. By Jacques Lacan. Edited by Jacques-Alain Miller. Translated by David Macey. New York: Verso, 2008, 126 pp., $16.95.

John Muller

John Muller

25 Main Street
Stockbridge, MA 01262
E-mail: john.muller@austenriggs.net

John Muller  

This slim volume, edited by Jacques-Alain Miller, Lacan's son-in-law, who has been editing the Seminars, consists of three brief talks Lacan gave in 1967 at separate locations in France. Two of them were previously unpublished; the other appeared in print in 1981, the year Lacan died. We may wonder what moved the editor to publish them now (the French edition appeared in 2005). The context back in 1967 included the recent publication of Lacan's substantial volume of writings, titled simply Écrits (1966); the first major conference on structuralism in America, held at Johns Hopkins in 1966; the publication of major works by Foucault (The Order of Things) and Derrida (Of Grammatology); and the appearance of a much-copied cartoon in a French periodical showing the “structuralists”—Lacan, Foucault, Barthes, and Lévi-Strauss—sitting under a palm tree. These things are evidently on Lacan's mind as he gives these three talks—but why should they concern us now, more than forty years later? Lacan provides a guide to the reader when he reminds us that he resurrected Freud's notion of Nachträglichkeit

Introduction

Steven Roose and Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford  

In this issue we introduce a new feature called Journal Watch. Much of the new research relevant to psychoanalytic constructs of mental function and the clinical situation is published in journals that are not routinely read by most clinicians. In fact, there are so many journals that it is not really possible for even the dedicated academic to keep up. We have therefore recruited members of the editorial board and assigned them each a group of journals to review searching for articles of interest to JAPA readers. The journals are the leading publications in the fields of psychology, psychiatry, neuroscience, social science, and medicine. As you will see, once an article is selected, a brief synopsis is prepared and full reference provided. Even with our best efforts we anticipate that there will be much we will miss. We welcome the help of JAPA readers and ask them, should they come across an article they think appropriate for Journal Watch, to contact Bret Rutherford, the editorial associate in charge of this section, at e-mail brr8@columbia.edu.

[[RunningHead]]

H.W. Koenigsberg, L.J. Siever, H. Lee, S. Pizzarello, A.S. New, M. Goodman, H. Cheng, J. Flory, & J. Prohovnik (2009). Neural correlates of emotion processing in borderline personality disorder.Psychiatry Research: Neuroimaging172:192-199.

Jeffrey Erbe and Eric Fertuck

Eric Fertuck

Eric Fertuck  

Emotional instability—particularly within personal relationships—is a cardinal feature of borderline personality disorder (BPD). Little is known about the neurobiological underpinnings of emotional instability in BPD. To investigate this phenomenon, Koenigsberg and colleagues (2009) conducted an experiment using standardized social-affective pictures to characterize neural responses in borderline patients.

143

Nineteen nonmedicated individuals with BPD and seventeen healthy volunteers were recruited to participate in the study. Functional MRI measured blood oxygenation levels during passive perception of emotionally valenced (positive and negative) pictures; following the scan, subjective ratings of the stimuli were collected. Though no significant differences were found between groups in the subjective ratings of the stimuli, the neuroimaging data revealed divergent activation patterns between BPD subjects (e.g., greater activity in primary visual areas, left amygdala, and right fusiform gyrus for negative pictures; superior temporal and inferior frontal gyri, posterior cingulate, and premotor areas for positive pictures) and healthy volunteers (e.g., greater activity in middle temporal and frontal gyri and insula for negative pictures; bilateral caudate for positive pictures).

The authors propose that the divergent neural activation to the emotional pictures reflected a “dual-process model.” The BPD neural activation pattern corresponds to “reflexive” emotion processing. Reflexive processing engages more phylogenetically primitive regions that activate quickly and without conscious thought to avert potentially dangerous threats. In contrast, the healthy volunteer pattern of neural activation suggests more “reflective” processing of emotional stimuli and is characterized by slower response, conscious awareness, and abstract contextualization of the emotional environment.

These results suggest that treatments for BPD could focus on helping the patient convert from reflexive to reflective emotional processing. Psychoanalytically based treatments for BPD, such as transference-focused psychotherapy (TFP) and mentalization-based therapy (MBT), specifically promote reflectiveness.

Marion Oliner  

The authors of this book draw on their extensive experience in areas in which the theme of vengeance has played a key role in mass destruction.1 They visited Rwanda after the genocide and have studied the effects of the hostility against foreigners in their own country, as well the effects of extreme traumatization. In addition, their work with couples has confronted them with the same ubiquitous motto: “Don't get mad, get even.”

The book is divided into parts dealing with three major topics: (1) vengeance and its causes; (2) acts of revenge; (3) renunciation of the recourse to revenge.

1Although they present themselves as coauthors, they have found it necessary to refer, in the middle of the text, to which of them has had the specific experience being discussed. I found this to be a distraction from the important subject they are addressing. To avoid confusion, I will discuss them as if in all instances they are truly coauthors.175

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Whose Analysis is it, Anyway? Commentary on Wilson

Eric Nuetzel

Eric Nuetzel

8820 Ladue Road, Suite 301
St. Louis, MO 63124
E-mail: ejnuetz@swbell.net

Eric Nuetzel   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Washington University School of Medicine; Honorary Professor of Psychology, Washington University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Saint Louis Psychoanalytic Institute.

Reading Mitchell Wilson's thoughtful and challenging paper, “Putting Practice into Theory,” puts me in mind of an incident from early in my career. I had just moved back to Saint Louis, my home, from New York City, where I had trained in psychiatry. I had accepted a full-time appointment at a local medical school. I was at a reception for new faculty members and was introduced to the dean. He asked me about my academic and research interests, and I told him of my intention to pursue psychoanalytic training. He commented on the requirement for a personal training analysis, and offered this observation: “Isn't the requirement just a way for the senior guys to keep their practices full?” Before I could say, “Actually, there are good reasons for the requirement, like …,” he had moved on. I wanted to tell him about all that I had yet to learn.

Twelve years after the incident with the dean, I was in Chicago meeting with a kind, elderly training analyst from the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis who was evaluating my work as part of the training and supervising analyst appointment process for the Saint Louis Psychoanalytic Institute. He asked, “Why do you want to be a training analyst?” I could have said, “Because I am interested in psychoanalytic education and in being generative,” but I did not. I said, without hesitating, “Because I want to practice analysis.” I wondered whether I had just confirmed my former dean's cynical view of the entire training analyst enterprise. Or was I simply being realistic?

The reality of my career as a training analyst for the past seventeen years has actually been more complex. I work and have worked with candidates, to be sure, but I have also worked with many in professions and fields far removed from mental health or even health care more generally.

313

Clinical Situations

Psychotherapeutic Approaches to Schizophrenic Psychoses: Past, Present and Future. Edited by Yrjö O. Alanen, Manuel González de Chávez, Ann-Louise S. Silver, and Brian Martindale. New York: Routledge, 2009, xx + 398 pp., $43.95.

Robert Stern

Robert Stern

340 Whitney Avenue
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: robert.stern@yale.edu

Robert Stern  

The International Society for the Psychological Treatments of Schizophrenias and other Psychoses (ISPS) is capping one hundred years in the history of psychotherapy for schizophrenic illnesses with the publication of this worldwide overview. The goal of the ISPS is to disseminate all aspects of knowledge on the psychotherapy of psychoses, “whose secrets almost certainly do not all reside in the neurosciences” (Martindale, p. viii). Indeed, our current understanding of the schizophrenias is that they are manifestations of disordered neurodevelopmental trajectories and circuits in the brain resulting from the interaction of a multiplicity of possible genetic events with a variety of potential environmental risk factors (Freedman 2010; Gilmore 2010; Tienari et al. 2004).

These pages bring together thirty-nine authors reporting on eleven European countries, the U.S., two East Asian countries, and New Zealand, giving historical and contemporary accounts of psychoanalytic and other psychological approaches to the treatment of schizophrenic people.

As is recounted here by Yrjö Alanen, Freud (1914) was pessimistic about the usefulness of psychoanalysis in the treatment of schizophrenia: “patients of this kind … display … diversion of their interest from the external world—from people and things. In consequence of the latter change, they become inaccessible to the influence of psycho-analysis and cannot be cured by our efforts” (p. 74). Despite Freud's misgivings, Paul Federn, one of his first analysands, began in 1906 to treat a schizophrenic female artist who, after being hospitalized, moved to Federn's house, where he continued her treatment, his wife acting as his therapeutic assistant.

1021

Steven Roose

Steven Roose

1051 Riverside Drive
New York, NY 10032
E-mail: spr2@columbia.edu

Steven Roose  

Neuroscientists believe that brain structure and function determine our perception and experience of the world. As psychoanalysts, we believe that perception and affective processing are critical for the development of the representational world and inner psychological life. These are not contradictory positions; they are just different perspectives. Indeed, what neuroscientists have demonstrated is something that psychoanalysts have always believed, namely, that not only do brain structures influence experience but experience also shapes brain structures. Plasticity of the brain is a capacity that exists in order for the individual to respond and adapt to the environment. This bidirectionality is probably most prominent in early life but continues throughout the life span. As Bruce Wexler states in Brain and Culture, “as a neuroscienctist and a psychiatrist, I draw on both experimental research and clinical experience as I explore the nature and implications of the rich, lifelong, and biologically mandated interaction between the individual and his or her physical and social environment. My conceptual starting point and foundation, however, are biological …” (p. 8). The circle of brain structure, perception, experience, and brain structure is a paradigm accepted by both neuroscientists and psychoanalysts. As we will see, this becomes problematic only when the author tries to extend this paradigm from the individual to society and culture.

The first chapter is a review of brain function; it is informative, well-written, and accessible to psychoanalysts not immersed in neuroscience. The next three chapters focus on illustrating “the great increase in functional variability among individuals that results from environmental influences on development of the brain” (p. 3) and the fact that human beings alter the environment that shapes their brains to a degree without precedent among animals. These chapters present concise and cogent summaries of several fascinating experiments.

Freud in Contexts

The Jewish World of Sigmund Freud: Essays on Cultural Roots and the Problem of Religious Identity. Edited by Arnold D. Richards, Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2010, 196 pp., $45.00.

Robert Burt

Robert Burt

P.O. Box 208215
New Haven, CT 06520
E-mail: robert.burt@yale.edu

Robert Burt  

In 2006, to mark the one-hundred-fiftieth anniversary of Freud's birth, a conference on “Freud's Jewish World” was held at the Center for Jewish History in New York. Judging by the papers published from that conference, it appears that Freud might not have welcomed this way of celebrating his birthday. It's not that Freud refused to acknowledge his Jewish origins. As Richard Armstrong observes in one of these essays, Freud “was too fiercely proud ever to deny his own Jewishness” (p. 36). Even if he had wanted to ignore these origins, anti-Semitism was too pervasive in Freud's Vienna to permit this course; and indeed, as many of the conference papers record, Freud saw on numerous occasions that social prejudice against Jews blocked his professional advancement. Even so, as these essays amply testify, Freud sought rigorously to exclude his Jewishness from his public self-presentation—and it is in this sense that Freud would not have been pleased that his birthday should be celebrated by placing him in a Jewish context.

The rigid secularism in Freud's public persona was clearly linked to his concern that psychoanalysis should not be seen as a “Jewish science.” Freud expressed this concern in a 1908 letter to Karl Abraham: “Our Aryan comrades are really completely indispensable to us, otherwise psychoanalysis would succumb to anti-Semitism” (p. 88). But more than social anti-Semitism was at stake in Freud's concern to attract Christian adherents. Freud insisted that there was nothing distinctively Jewish about psychoanalysis as such—that its premises were based on universal characteristics of human mentation, and that its status as a science depended on this universalism.

Breaking Up Blues: A Guide to Survival and Growth. By Denise Cullington. Sussex, UK: Routledge, 2008, 296 pp., $79.95 hardcover, $18.00 paperback.

Joan Wexler

Joan Wexler

255 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06510
E-mail: joan.wexler@yale.edu

Joan Wexler  

Breaking Up Blues: A Guide to Survival and Growth is a self-help book about mourning the end of a marriage or long-term partnership. The author

614

describes the breakups of heterosexual couples; and while she says that gay couples will find the book helpful as well, her chapter on childhood development describes primarily heterosexual development. The book is useful for couples with or without children and includes a substantial number of pages on the impact of divorce on children. But this is a self-help book with a difference. A quick perusal of other self-help books on divorce reveals this one to be unique in its comprehensiveness. It is well-written, thoughtful, and both psychoanalytically and personally informed. Its author, Denise Cullington, a British psychologist and psychoanalyst, creates a map through the difficult emotional territory of breaking up. She has a clear-eyed vision of the enormous challenges and painful feelings that breaking up engenders but also offers perspective as she reminds readers that while they are navigating very difficult territory, divorce is not Rwanda or the Holocaust. Early on she reveals that her own marriage broke up. So this book may well be the one she would have wanted to read during her own marital crisis. In her acknowledgments she writes, “Finally, this book would not have been written without the many good years with Tom, the ending of which left me with the curious problem of how to grapple with, and move on from them” (p. x).

Part 1, Prelude to Break-up, has three chapters after an introduction: “Should You Leave?” “Should You Stay?” and “Being Left.” Part 2, The Trauma of Break-up, has chapters titled “Loss,” “Rage,” “Legal Battles,” “Refusing to Mind,” and “Depression.” Her focus is the internal work required through and out of a divorce. She emphasizes that breaking up is a slow process. The structure of the book is relevant to the process she describes. In the first two parts, Cullington describes how a couple feel whipped through turbulent storms of powerful and volatile feelings. Overwhelmed by the conviction each has of being wounded or right, they are unable to think, let alone discuss what is happening. Her description offers the reader straightforward language, desperately needed, to define the emotional riptides for both leaver and left.

Paradoxes of the Self: The Contrapuntal Style of Arnold Modell

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: lewis_kirshner@hms.harvard.edu

Lewis Kirshner   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychonanalytic Institute.

The work of Arnold Modell over the past forty years constitutes a major contribution to contemporary psychoanalytic thought. Of particular note are his efforts to sustain a paradoxical conception of the self as both an evolving, contingent product and an enduring core. Moving back and forth between celebration of the private self and articulation of the impossibility of a one-person psychoanalysis, Modell's quest to define the nature of the self has taken him from classical analytic theory, through Winnicott and object relations, to the philosophy of intersubjectivity and, in later years, to the work of infant researchers and neuroscientists. In doing so, he has placed the continuity of the self and the making of personal meaning at the center of psychoanalytic practice. The evolution of his thinking reflects a turn toward a protean and transitional conception of the self, away from the notion of an enduring or superordinate core self.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street

Aaron Esman  

Framed by introduction and epilogue by its editors, this volume assembles some sixteen contributions to a conference organized by Peter Rudnytsky in 2004, bringing together a diverse group of scholars and practitioners to explore and clarify the commonalities and boundaries between two disciplines concerned with the care of troubled persons. Each essay in its own way addresses the roles of telling, hearing, and reading stories—“narratives”—in the healing process; ultimately, it serves as a challenging plea for a more humanistic medicine founded on the physician's empathic, respectful, and—yes—unhurried attention to each patient's story of his illness and its place in his life history.

As is usual in such multiauthored collections, the depth and quality of the papers vary, though I must say that for me, new to the idea of “narrative medicine” as a definable field, most were illuminating and often quite moving. In her opening paper, Rita Charon, a psychoanalytically informed internist who directs a program in narrative medicine at Columbia (and the book's coeditor), traces the roots of the subject in “literature, the so-called medical humanities, primary care, relation-centered care, patient-centered care, and biopsychosocial medicine. … they all in one way or another circle around language, telling, relation and the imagination. … narrative medicine designate[s] medicine practiced with narrative competence to acknowledge the urgency of time, to value the singularity of patients and self, to seek the causality and to tolerate the savage [!

Documentary Films

Between Madness and Art. A film by Christian Beetz. Icarus Films, 75 minutes.Hidden Gifts: The Mystery of Angus MacPhee. A film by Nick Higgins. Icarus Films, 25 minutes.

Janice Lieberman

Janice Lieberman

55 East 87th Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: janicelieberman@gmail.com

Janice Lieberman  

The recent release of two DVDs, Between Madness and Art and Hidden Gifts: The Mystery of Angus MacPhee, presents us an opportunity to assess the value of film as compared with books and journal articles as a medium through which to communicate a psychoanalytic understanding of the creative process. The artists featured in these films were confined to European mental hospitals without revolving door policies. In the publicity materials sent out with the DVD, the producers of the first film ask, “What is the link between psychological states and the creative process? Is there a relationship between psychosis and the artistic impulse? What can artworks produced by mental patients tell us about artistic genius? Can art therapy be helpful in the treatment of the mentally ill?” Relevant discussions and debates in the psychoanalytic literature on what is being worked through by the artist in his artmaking can be found in Kris (1952), Greenacre (1971), Rosegrant (1987), MacGregor (1989), Knafo (2002), Maurer (2004), and Esman (2006).

In these films the interrelationships between mental illness and the making of art, what today we call “outsider art” or “art brut,” are examined by way of interviews with psychiatrists, caretakers, family members, and the mentally ill artists themselves. The artworks are shown in numbers that would be impossible in print due to considerations of cost and space. In my research I have learned that “outsider art” has had waves of great popularity and interest, and that Intuit: The Center for Intuitive and Outside Art was founded in Chicago in 1991. In New York, the Museum of American Folk Art features the work of these unusual untrained artists. A well-attended Outsider Arts Fair has been held annually since 1992, and there are many collectors. Henry Darger and Bill Traylor are two of the more famous of these artists.

Between Madness and Art, a 75-minute film by Christian Beetz (who has won awards in Germany), was released by Icarus Films in 2008.

[[RunningHead]]

Can Psychiatry Residents Be Attracted to Analytic Training? A Survey of Five Residency Programs

Debra Katz and Marcia Kaplan

Marcia Kaplan

3470 Blazer Parkway
Lexington, KY 40509
E-mail: dkatzO@emaiI.uky.edu

Marcia Kaplan   Training and Supervising Analyst, Cincinnati Psychoanalytic Institute; Professor of Clinical Psychology (volunteer), Department of Psychiatry, University of Cincinnati College of Medicine.

In the face of fewer psychiatrist applicants for psychoanalytic training, determining the interest of current psychiatric residents in psychoanalysis and psychodynamic psychiatry is a pressing concern. To gauge this interest, an anonymous online survey was sent to residents from five psychiatry residency programs in the Midwest and South. Seventy-five residents responded, for a return rate of 42%. The data suggest that residents value psychoanalytic concepts and most plan to incorporate the practice of psychodynamic psychotherapy into their careers after graduation; however, residents have little confidence in their level of skill and the adequacy of their training. While 46% express interest in further psychodynamic psychotherapy training, only 22% express interest in psychoanalytic training. Most cite the cost and time involved as reasons they would not pursue further training. This study demonstrates that psychiatric residents have strong interest in and respect for psychodynamic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. The data suggest that psychiatric residents are a viable pool of applicants for psychoanalytic training, especially if barriers to training can be reduced and creative ways for psychoanalysts to engage residents can be fostered.

Sexual Excitement: Dynamics of Erotic Life. By Robert J. Stoller. New York: Pantheon Books, 1979, 281 pp.

David Fisher

David Fisher

1800 Fairburn Avenue, Suite 203
Los Angeles, CA 90025
E-mail: djamesfisher@aol.com

David Fisher  

In revisiting the case history of Belle, the centerpiece of Robert Stoller's 1979 book, Sexual Excitement: Dynamics of Erotic Life, I will present three vignettes.

First, twinkling thighs: On the couch during her analytic hours, Belle would manipulate the bottom of her skirt so that it moved up and down her thighs. Sometimes her skirt would be hiked up high enough that her bottom, as she called it, would be seen. In Stoller's words, “I would gradually become aware that first her knees and then an inch or more of her thigh were twinkling at me while at the same time she chattered away” (p. 131). Belle sometimes came to analysis wearing no underpants.

Second, the Erotic Daydream, sometimes referred to as the Director's Fantasy: “A cruel man, the Director, a Nazi type, is directing the activity. It consists of Belle being raped by a stallion, which has been aroused to a frenzy by a mare held off at a distance beyond where Belle is placed. In a circle around the periphery stand vaguely perceived men, expressionless, masturbating while ignoring each other, the Director, and Belle. She is there for the delectation of these men, including the Director, who although he has an erection, makes no contact with her: her function is to be forced to unbearable sexual excitement and pleasure, thereby making a fool of herself before these men. She has been enslaved in this obscene exhibition of humiliation because it creates erections in these otherwise feelingless men; they stand there in phallic, brutal indifference. All that, however, is foreplay, setting the scene.

Editors' note: This book essay is dedicated to the memory of Gladys Topkis (d. August 2009), a friend of psychoanalysis, a dear colleague, and senior editor at Yale University Press until 1999. Robert Stoller, some of whose books she edited, was one of her favorite authors.

Anne Sexton's Recorded Therapy: Commentary on Skorczewski

Paula Salvio

Paula Salvio

Durham, NH 03823
E-mail: pmsalvio@gmail.com

Paula Salvio   Professor of Education, Graduate Faculty, College of Liberal Arts, University of New Hampshire.

I don't think genius and insanity grow in the same bed…. I think the artist must have a heightened awareness. It is only seldom that this sprouts from mental illness alone.

—Anne Sexton (Marx 1965-1966, p. 561)

I used to describe [writing poetry] like this; that if you used form it was like letting a lot of wild animals out of the arena, but enclosing them in a cage, and you could let some extraordinary animals out if you had the right cage, and that cage would be the form.

—Anne Sexton (Marx 1965-1966, p. 568)

“I' who reportedly write so truthfully about myself, so openly, am not that open,” wrote Anne Sexton about keeping a journal for the Teachers and Writers Collaborative in 1967. Despite her reputation for prying open her family album and writing about addictions, divorce, adultery, and the myths encrypted in what she described as the Gothic New England family romance, Sexton winced at descriptions that cast her as a confessional poet. “I'm often being personal,” she explained to her students at Colgate, “but I'm not being personal about myself,” suggesting with characteristic irony and prescience that the personal is already a plural condition.

Sexton was often described by critics as “primitive,” and criticized for using poetry to act out her personal problems, her “small wounds,” resorting to what the critic Robert Boyers (1974) described as “excessive self-dramatization, even spilling into undertones of self-pity” (p. 207). James Dickey criticized her for dwelling “on the pathetic and disgusting aspects of bodily experience” (quoted in Ostriker 1988, p. 264)

responseSet.title: Two Surveys: An Introduction
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two Surveys: An Introduction

Robert Glick

Robert Glick

E-mail: rag4@columbia.edu

Robert Glick  

The surveys in this Psychoanalytic Education Section explore two serious problems facing analytic educators, candidates, and potential candidates. The first addresses a tension between the apparently robust interest in psychoanalytic knowledge among psychiatric residents in parts of the U.S. and their declining recruitment to psychoanalytic training. The second addresses the perception of potential difficulties facing candidates in the training experience at the British Psychoanalytical Society. While conducted in seemingly disparate contexts, these surveys allow us to better assess significant obstacles to the dissemination of analytic knowledge and skill.

In “Can Psychiatry Residents Be Attracted to Analytic Training? A Survey of Five Residency Programs,” Debra Katz and Marcia Kaplan set the context for their study with an incisive overview of current recruitment and practice problems in the field. They then present an anonymous survey of five psychiatric residency programs (in Ohio, Indiana, and Kentucky) designed to assess residents' exposure to psychoanalytic knowledge and their interest in it, particularly with regard to career planning. With 42% of residents responding, they found a very strong interest in psychodynamic thinking and psychodynamic psychotherapy, limited exposure to working psychoanalysts, little confidence in the psychodynamic clinical skills acquired in their residency, and modest interest in analytic training (time and cost were cited as major barriers). Several important questions are raised by this survey: Has psychiatry lost interest in psychodynamic and psychoanalytic ideas and practice? What do residents who receive limited exposure to these ideas base their apparent enthusiasm on? What do they know, and how do they learn it? Are cost and time “real” or major barriers to a career interest in analysis? More studies such as this one may energize our educators into creative action.

In “Between Paranoia and Creativity: Candidates' Experience of Psychoanalytic Training,” Anne Ward, Walter Gibson, and Christine Miqueu-Baz explore the question of whether power and authority issues compromise the educational experience of candidates in training at the British Society.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Putting Practice into Theory: Making the Training Analyst System Coherent

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

2960 Pledmont Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94705
E-mail: mdwmd@comcast.net

Mitchell Wilson   Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

The question of the training analysis demands a complicated set of answers that engage the issue at different levels of human organization. Historically, the training analysis has been the central feature of the tripartite model of psychoanalytic education. Internal and external pressures have burdened the training analysis and called its legitimacy into question. This problem of legitimacy amounts to a lack of coherence in the training analyst (TA) system. This lack engenders idealized fantasies of the role of the TA, in which the TA embodies special talents and attributes, and of the system that sanctions that role. This idealization is haunted by its opposite: a melancholic devaluation of psychoanalysis and a fear that it will collapse. Recent literature on the analyst's position in the psychoanalytic process emphasizes the analyst's position as decentered and conflicted. The analyst's decentered, conflicted status goes against this idealizing impulse. An attempt is made to wed analytic values, and what we know about the analyst's role in the analytic process, into a more coherent, consistent position regarding the analysis of candidates.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Comparison of the Dependent Variables in CBT and PDT Outcome Research: Toward Measuring Symptom States, Personality Traits, and Mental Capacities

Alla Gordon and Sanjay Nath

Sanjay Nath

1259 South Cedar Crest Boulevard, Suite 325
Allentown, PA 18103
E-mail: agordon@rcn.com

Sanjay Nath  

This poster is based on a dissertation presented by Alla Gordon to the faculty of the School of Human Service Professions of Widener University in

556

partial fulfillment of the requirements for the degree of doctor of psychology (Sanjay Nath, dissertation advisor).

To discover how theoretical assumptions determine dependent measures and the concept of change, this study compared the outcome research of cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) with that of psychodynamic therapies (PDT). The latter category includes all psychodynamic and psychoanalytic therapies, as well as psychoanalysis. CBT and PDT were chosen for comparison because they represent opposite epistemological assumptions regarding personality.

The study's primary hypotheses are that CBT outcome studies will mostly measure decreases in symptom states and will rarely focus on personality traits or increases in mental capacities, while outcome studies of PDT (operationally defined as any therapy that targets the locus of pathology in deficits and conflicts in the dynamic unconscious) will tend to measure both symptom state reduction, personality trait changes (as found in personality disorders and structure), and increased mental capacities.

Method: Selection of Studies

Empirical pre-post treatment outcome research (on both efficacy and effectiveness) was selected from CBT and PDT outcome studies found in PsycINFO. PsycINFO is an electronic bibliographic database that provides abstracts and articles of the scholarly literature in psychology and related fields and contains more than 2.6 million records. Although all search engines have flaws and inherent selection biases, PsycINFO was chosen not only as a source, but also as a method of selection. This was to help reduce experimenter subjectivity in the initial selection of studies. Also, any search engine errors should be equally distributed to the independent variables.

The following criteria were used in the literature search: (1) theoretical orientation (“CBT” or “psychoanal*” or “psychodynamic”) and “treatment outcome” (both as “any field” type of search); (2) peer-reviewed journals only (thus excluding dissertations and book chapters); (3) the publication years 1998-2007 inclusive.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Treating Patients who are Parents: The Good Grandparent Transference and Countertransference

Ruth Imber

Ruth Imber

451 West End Avenue, Suite 1-J
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: rimberphd@aol.com

Ruth Imber   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, William Alanson White Institute.

Psychoanalysis has paid scant attention to the role of grandparenthood, especially as it may appear in treatment. Clinical material is presented to illustrate the transference-countertransference phenomenon of what will be called the good grandparent; this is not an uncommon occurrence in the treatment of patients who are parents. But while the occurrence of a grandparent transference may occur with some frequency, it is not always recognized as such. When patients require support, mirroring, and general encouragement for their parenting skills, they may be demonstrating a legitimate need that should not be rejected out of hand by the analyst. At the same time, such an apparently benign process may mask negative feelings and fantasies to which we must remain alert.

[[RunningHead]]

Therapy after Terror: 9/11, Psychotherapists, and Mental Health. By Karen M. Seeley. New York: Cambridge University Press, 2008, 242 pp., $35.00.

Bennett Simon

Bennett Simon

44B Burroughs Street
Jamaica Plain, MA 02130
E-mail: Bennett_simon@hms.harvard.edu

Bennett Simon  

Read this book. Karen Seeley's Therapy after Terror: 9/11, Psychotherapists, and Mental Health is uniquely disturbing and challenging among the important books on treating the populations and individuals affected by the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks on the World Trade Center and the Pentagon (see, e.g., Coates, Rosenthal, and Schechter 2003; Danieli and Dingman 2005; see also Apfel and Simon 2005). Seeley has chosen to examine the situation in New York, where she lives and works, and not deal with Washington, D.C., or Shanksville, Pennsylvania, the site of the crash of the plane intended to attack the White House or Capitol.

Several parts of her career come together in this book: a clinician (in private practice, and formerly on the staff of the Furman Counseling Center at Barnard College), a teacher in the Psychology Department at Barnard, a professor in the Anthropology Department at Columbia, and author of a monograph, Cultural Psychotherapy: Working with Culture in the Clinical Encounter (Seeley 2000).

The core of the work that went into this new book is her interviews with thirty-five mental health professionals who had been actively involved in the response to the September 11 disaster. She did not attempt any systematic sampling of therapists; these thirty-five were available and willing to be interviewed. It appears that most of the interviews were conducted in a single sitting (the length and nature of the interviews are not described), but some included later follow-up interviews. The thirty-five included psychologists, social workers, and psychiatrists, and half the group “had additional training in psychoanalysis.” Those interviews were augmented by Seeley's attending larger gatherings of therapists, and by her involvement in Psychotherapists for Social Responsibility.

responseSet.authorMast: Filipa de Castro,  Nora Hinojosa Ayala and  Linda C. Mayes

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Selected Posters from the 2010 Annual Poster Session: Maternal Attributions of Intentionality: Measuring Adaptive and Pathological Maternal Projections

Filipa de Castro , Nora Hinojosa Ayala and Linda Mayes

Linda Mayes

7a Cda Fray Pedro Gante #50
Colonia Seccion XVI, Tlalpan
Mexico DF CP14000
MEXICO
E-mail: fcastro@insp.mx

Linda Mayes  

Infants are born with limited capacity for autoregulation and rely on the primary caregiver to modulate their affective states. This interpersonal regulatory process depends on the maternal capacity to correctly interpret the infant's cues. The process of interpreting the infant's cues involves, among other things, the attribution of intentions to the baby and depends on appropriate levels of maternal preoccupation (Winnicott 1960), emotional containment (Bion 1959), empathy, and reflective function (Fonagy 1995). Our research emphasizes how the creation of internal meaning is influenced and shaped from an early age by this emotional relationship with the primary caregivers through the mechanisms of projection, introjection, and projective identification. These interpersonal and intrapsychic mechanisms play a universal role in perception and behavior, which can be observed in a developmental continuum from adaptive forms of empathy to pathological levels of delusion (Zinner and de Castro 2006). Although a considerable amount of work has been done both on the clinical aspects of projection and projective identification and on the regulatory mechanisms taking place in the early mother-baby relationship (Lieberman 1999; Seligman 1999; Silverman and Lieberman 1999; Alvarez 1999; Grotstein 1999), as well as in the family as a whole, there is little empirical work and systematic research evidence on the rather complex dynamic mechanisms involved in the failure of these processes. In our research we suggest that early maternal projections can be empirically investigated by measuring the positive (adaptive) and/or negative (pathological) attributions of intentionality to the baby.

responseSet.authorMast: Tim Rice and  Andrew J. Gerber
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L.J. Young (2009). Being human: Love: neuroscience reveals all.Nature457(7226): 148, January 8.

Tim Rice and Andrew Gerber

Andrew Gerber

Andrew Gerber  

Psychoanalysis has long appreciated the complicated relationship between maternal and romantic love. Researchers are now studying this very phenomenon by elucidating the psychobiology of pair-bonding. A January 2009 opinion article in Nature by the social neurobiologist Larry Young details his own work on the bonding patterns of prairie voles, a small rodent similar to the hamster, and also cites studies in humans that report findings consistent with clinical psychoanalytic observations.

Much of Young's work has focused on the hormone oxytocin. In an earlier publication (Young and Wang 2004), he reported that in the female prairie vole higher concentrations of the oxytocin receptor in the brain reward network, specifically the nucleus accumbens, correlated with stronger maternal-infant bonding. When these receptors were blocked via an oxytocin antagonist, bonding ceased. Earlier work had shown a similar correlation in adult sexual partner selection: central oxytocin infusion stimulated selection, while antagonist blockade prevented such mate-bonding. Most strikingly, when the subjects are switched from rodents to humans, fMRI studies show that mothers viewing photographs of their children activate reward system areas nearly identical to those activated in lovers viewing images of their mates. The finding that similar brain areas are activated during attachment and sexual relationships is of compelling interest to psychoanalysts.

Young suggests other ways in which this work can be extended to human relationships. For example, he cites a finding by Walum et al. (2008) that variations in the vasopressin receptor (the vasopressin system being the oxytocin pair-bonding system's analogue in the male vole) are associated with the varying propensity to monogamous behavior in humans. There appears to be an association between the activity of the vasopressin receptor gene and an estimation of marital satisfaction. However, it is important to note that all of these studies report associations, not cause and effect. We know that brain activity creates experiences, while experiences shape the brain as well.

Process and outcomes of psychotherapists' personal therapy: Replications and extension 20 years later.Psychotherapy: Theory, Research, Practice, Training46:19-31.J. Norcross, D. Bike, & K. Evans (2009). The therapist's therapist: A replication and extension 20 years later.Psychotherapy: Theory, Research, Practice, Training46:32-41.

Stephanie Gold and Mark Hilsenroth

Mark Hilsenroth

Mark Hilsenroth  

Two recent articles by John Norcross and colleagues replicate and extend earlier research on the process and outcomes of psychotherapists' personal therapy experiences. In one study, 727 randomly selected mental health professionals (261 psychologists, 232 counselors, and 234 social workers) were asked to describe their own treatment. The rate of personal therapy varied significantly among therapists of different theoretical orientations, with psychodynamic, psychoanalytic, interpersonal, and humanistic therapists the most likely to have received personal therapy and those with cognitive, behavioral, and multicultural orientations least likely. Not surprisingly, therapists in psychoanalysis or psychoanalytic therapy spent significantly more time in treatment on average than therapists who sought other types of treatment. Approximately 24 percent of the sample used psychotropic medication in conjunction with personal therapy. The top four reasons reported for seeking therapy were marital-couple distress, depression, need for self-understanding, and anxiety/stress. In addition to therapeutic effect, all respondents viewed personal therapy as an important prerequisite for clinical practice and ongoing career development.

The majority of the sample chose personal therapists from their own profession and theoretical framework.

Trained in Buenos Aires as a physician and psychoanalyst, Héctor Fiorini, a professor at Buenos Aires University, founder and director of the Center for Studies in Psychotherapies, has written extensively. Fiorini has been deeply interested in a variety of approaches to the suffering mind in its various expressions and is dedicated to their careful study. He has devoted himself as well to studying the creative process in art and life. His studies have been enriched by extensive multidisciplinary research. Indeed, the extensive references in his work attest his respectful listening to philosophers, painters, musicians, writers, actors, directors, and filmmakers, the sources of his work on creativity.

One of his most interesting ideas about the creative process is that it involves a temporality different from that of Freud's primary and secondary processes. Transcending successive time, unconscious atemporality, and Nachträglichkeit

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Lexicography: Notes from Two “Harmless Drudges”

Eslee Samberg and Elizabeth Auchincloss

Elizabeth Auchincloss

165 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: esamberg@gmail.com

15 West 81st Street
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: elauchin@cornell.med.com

Elizabeth Auchincloss   Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, Cornell University Department of Psychiatry; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

The co-Editors in Chief of the American Psychoanalytic Association's new edition of Psychoanalytic Terms and Concepts (previously edited by Moore and Fine and last revised in 1990) recount their lexicographical adventures. Editing a dictionary at the turn of the twenty-first century is a daunting, some might say foolhardy, undertaking. The most obvious challenge faced by the editors was the growing pluralism within psychoanalysis. However, a more fundamental challenge was that the object of psychoanalytic study, the mind and its processes, can be known only by putting words to our observations, inferences, and interpretations. Psychoanalytic thinkers, starting with Freud, have wrestled with this challenge in ways that define the history of psychoanalysis itself. Long gone are the days when Freud could commend the “correctness” of Sterba's lexicographical efforts. Today postmodern critics, at the opposite extreme, argue that terms and concepts are best understood as “verbal gestures” in the “language-game” of psychoanalysis. Some go so far as to assert that dictionary-writing is obsolete. The co-Editors in Chief of Psychoanalytic Terms and Concepts have not succumbed to such nihilistic views, but have instead struggled to establish a reasonable stance within contemporary debates over the nature of psychoanalytic language.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Worthless Female Material”: Nursemaids and Governesses in Freud's Cases

Daria Colombo

Daria Colombo

4033 East Madison Street, Suite 107
Seattle, WA 98112
E-mail: dcolombo8@gmail.com

Daria Colombo   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Washington; faculty, Seattle Psychoanalytic Institute; faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

The figure of the governess, central in Freud's own history, is present in most of his cases. Freud described his nursemaid as “the prime originator” of his neuroses. Well after Freud's abandonment of the seduction theory, female servants were consistently portrayed as seducing boys, while their relationship with girls consisted of identification and rivalry. The role of Freud's own surrogate mother in his life and writing is examined, and two cases, Lucy R. and Dora, are looked at through the lens of female caretaking. A review and integration of relevant literature is followed by an exploration of the perplexing adherence in Freud's writing to the reality of seduction by a governess, even after he had abandoned the seduction theory. It is argued that it is in the figure of the female maid that the “shadowy” early history of Freud's mothering experiences may actually be engaged, however indirectly and unconsciously. This figure, and Freud's powerful, problematic identification with her, is a thread that when pulled helps unravel struggles in Freud's early theoretical development around issues of female sexuality and analytic authority. Revisiting Lucy R. and Dora provides a new perspective on Freud's difficulty with maternal transferences, and restores to their original importance his “worthless” governesses, those first analysts.

[[RunningHead]]

The Role of the Couch in Psychoanalysis: Proposed Research Designs and Some Preliminary Data

Ira Lable , John Kelley , Julie Ackerman , Raymond Levy , Sherwood Waldron and J. Ablon

J. Ablon

15 Parkman Street WACC812
Boston, MA 021 14
E-mail: ira-lable@hms.harvard.edu

J. Ablon   Clinical Associate Professor, Massachusetts General Hospital and Harvard Medical School; Director, Psychotherapy Research Program, Massachusetts General Hospital.

Ever since Freud, the couch has been viewed as an important—some would argue essential—component of psychoanalysis. Although many theoretical papers and case reports have addressed the use of the couch in psychoanalysis, no empirical study has investigated its effect on psychoanalytic process or outcome. After a review of the literature, a number of research designs are proposed that might be used in such an investigation. Finally, preliminary empirical data are presented from archived audiotapes of two psychoanalyses: one in which the patient switched from lying down to sitting up, and one in which the opposite occurred. The aim is to stimulate research-oriented psychoanalysts to undertake empirical investigations of the theoretical concepts underlying use of the couch and, more generally, to present a specific example of research as a paradigm for a broader research agenda for empirical investigation of the key theoretical ideas underlying psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Developing Psychoanalytic Cases and the Candidates Who will Analyze Them: An Educational Initiative

Arden Rothstein

Arden Rothstein

275 Central Park West, 1C
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: aroth275@aol.com

Arden Rothstein   Training and Supervising Analyst and Chair, Academic Affairs, NYU Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, NYU School of Medicine.

An educational initiative, Psychoanalytic Case Development Supervision, began as an ad hoc practical response when nearly half the candidates in a talented class lacked the psychoanalytic case required for progression. All incoming candidates were assigned a supervisor with whom to meet weekly to consider clinic applicants and patients in their psychotherapy practices for analysis. Gradually it has become clear that readying candidates, in highly individualized ways, to engage in intensive work is as important as developing patients. Not only has this program yielded beneficial results for progression; it has also contributed to a subtle paradigm shift in pedagogy centering on two contemporary controversies: objective assessment of analyzability vs. subjectivity in recommending analysis, and the value of a categorical distinction, based on frequency of sessions and use of the couch, between psychoanalysis and psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy. A four-year process of discovery—drawing on interviews with forty-nine participants (educators, supervisors, candidates)—is contextualized within the literature on these controversies. Participants' reflections are discussed, as are other features of the program and future directions for this work in progress.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Implications of Contemporary Gender Theory

Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

625 Purdy Street
Birmingham, MI 48009
E-mail: nkulish@aol.com

Nancy Kulish   Adjunct Professor, Department of Psychology, Wayne State University; Adjunct Assistant Professor of Psychology, University of Detroit/Mercy; Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute. Submitted for publication March 7, 2010.

The current intellectual scene in psychoanalysis is marked by vigorous theoretical controversies about gender. The ideas being debated have important implications for clinical work, which have not been thoroughly explicated or integrated into common practice. These implications include the following: gender can accrue idiosyncratic meanings; gender identity is considered fluid and rigidity of gender identity deemed problematic; gender-related conflicts are typically described as divergent; analysis of superego conflicts related to gender becomes particularly important; and, finally, gender-related biases are seen as inevitable and must be taken into account in the clinical situation. A detailed clinical example illustrates the application of these ideas. While the more dramatic cases related to gender have been more frequent subjects of study, conflicts about gender are everyday occurrences for our patients and deserve further attention.

responseSet.title: Life is Not a Dream: The Importance of Being Real
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Life is Not a Dream: The Importance of Being Real

Marion Oliner

Marion Oliner

670 West End Avenue #9F
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: marionoliner@earthlink.net

Marion Oliner  

This paper explores the value attached to “being real,” how it is used in various contexts, and how this value persists outside the purview of reality testing or other objective criteria. It addresses how we view the form of a dream and other illusions of reality, including fetishes, whose sole value resides in their material reality. With Proust's description of the experience of tasting a madeleine (compared with the indistinctness of highly charged emotional memories) and a clinical incident as examples, it is possible to assess the important role the search for perceptual identity plays in making the past come to life. Because this search stems from a period of development earlier than the striving for thought identity, it has wrongly attained the status of a poor relative. To counter this, an effort is made to demonstrate its implicit value as a motive underlying repetition. Because in analysis insistence on the reality of an experience is frequently used defensively, analysts run the danger of joining patients in treating external and psychic reality as dichotomous, thereby underestimating the beneficial potential of being real for limiting unconscious omnipotence and thereby restoring from oblivion the past and the wider context of an experience.
