[[RunningHead]]

Beyond the Dyad: Individual Psychology, Social World

Nancy Chodorow

Nancy Chodorow

7 Meadow Way
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: nancy_chodorow@hms.harvard.edu

Nancy Chodorow   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Lecturer on Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Professor of Sociology Emerita, UC Berkeley.

In recent years analytic writing, presenting, and clinical discussion almost universally accord attention to the analytic dyad. My reflections here propose two kinds of reservation about this ubiquitous focus. On the one hand, it leads us away from the richness and complexity of the patient's intrapsychic life—her clinical individuality—which psychoanalysis alone among the therapies works to expand. On the other hand, we err in thinking that the dyad is isolated, when in fact it is always situated beyond itself. My subject brings together my identity and personal passions as a psychoanalyst and my origins in sociology and anthropology.

We can trace the history of this global march toward the dyad. First, our understanding of therapeutic action changed. Freud had discovered transference and argued that it was inevitable and indeed a resource: remembering requires repeating, and you cannot destroy someone in effigy (Freud 1912, 1914). Over time, anchored by Strachey (1934), analysts extended Freud to think that unless you were analyzing the transference in the here and now, real change could not happen. Working in the transference became a sine qua non of analytic action and the real work of analysis.

This view noticed the patient's mind alone. In the world of classical ego psychology, in which many American psychoanalysts grew up, clinical theory and the theory of technique taught that the analyst was an objective interpreter of the patient's psyche, through reconstruction, dream interpretation, pointing to conflict, resistances, and defenses, and especially through observing and interpreting the transference, which the patient brought from the there and then to the here and now. Attending to

Plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 15, 2010. The author thanks Glen Gabbard, Theodore Jacobs, Ellen Pinsky, and Barrie Thorne, for reading drafts of this address and making valuable suggestions, and APsaA's Committee on Research and Special Training (CORST), for enabling her to write it as both analyst and academic.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Ambivalence about Continuing and Deepening an Analysis

Lena Ehrlich

Lena Ehrlich

555 East William, Suite 22C
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: lenaehrlich@comcast.net

Lena Ehrlich   Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; adjunct faculty, Department of Psychiatry, University of Michigan.

Of the many variables that affect an analyst's capacity to analyze, the analyst's ambivalence about analysis has received minimal attention. A previous paper (Ehrlich 2004) addressed how the analyst's ambivalence manifests itself in reluctance to recommend analysis and address the patient's resistances to the recommendation. The present paper examines the analyst's ambivalence about maintaining and deepening the analytic engagement. More specifically, it explores the analyst's conflicts around maintaining the analytic frame. A detailed clinical example illustrates how the analyst's continued scrutiny of her reluctance to maintain the analytic frame allowed a continuation of the analysis and a deepening of the analytic process. Given the current economic crisis and patients' increased propensity to use concerns about fee and frequency as a mode of resistance, it becomes increasingly important for analysts to recognize how their own ambivalence undermines their capacity to address those resistances effectively.

[[RunningHead]]

Behind the Couch: Uses and Misuses of Temptation

Wendy Jacobson

Wendy Jacobson

2250 North Druid Hills Road, Suite 233
Atlanta, GA 30329
E-mail: wjaco02@emory.edu

Wendy Jacobson  

Sharply divided opinions characterized this panel, making for an interesting afternoon. The issue at stake was the optimal analytic attitude and technical approach to the inevitable “temptations” and “minor transgressions” that occur in the daily work of psychoanalysts. The panelists fell into two camps. Stanley Coen, organizer and chair, and Joyce Slochower, both from New York, drew a firm line, feeling analysts do not consistently “grasp” or “catch” themselves in their minor lapses so they can reflect on what they have done or been tempted to do. This camp views such lapses as critically important signals, given their potential to stalemate treatments. In contrast, Michael Parsons of London and Dominique Scarfone of Montreal saw the questions posed by the panel as too narrow and missing the point of what the analytic endeavor needs to be about. Hence the panel provided an eloquent debate over what internal environment on the part of the analyst facilitates the most useful kind of analytic listening, immersion, creativity, and outcome.

Coen put forth the questions to be addressed: How do we decide what is and isn't appropriate for us as analysts to experience as we sit behind the couch with our patients? How far can we let ourselves go in our wishes, fantasies, and temptations at work? What are the limits of our desires as analysts? How much sexual temptation should we feel with an attractive patient in an erotic transference? How sexually aroused should we become? How much detachment, distance, or lack of concern should we feel with a schizoid patient who needs to maintain distance? How much should we want to share in a narcissistically impressive patient's specialness, talent, and celebrity? How much murderous hatred should we feel with an angry, rejecting, negativistic patient? How much of what we imagine aims to satisfy our own idiosyncratic needs; how much has to do with our patients' needs? How do we decide whether our idiosyncratic needs are somehow interconnected with the ongoing analytic process?

[[RunningHead]]

Treatment Preparatory to Psychoanalysis: A Reconsideration after Twenty-Five Years

Stephen Bernstein

Stephen Bernstein

146 Wallis Road
Chestnut Hill, MA 02467
E-mail: sbb@massmed.org

Stephen Bernstein   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Tufts University School of Medicine; Lecturer in Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

The author's initial article on treatment preparatory to psychoanalysis (1983) challenged the long-held belief that a therapy with the same analyst would contaminate a subsequent analytic transference. The current article reconsiders the original process of transition from therapy to analysis and describes methods that can further its effectiveness. Although specific noninterpretive interventions to enhance preparation for analysis are rarely written about, they are discussed among colleagues and in supervision. Levy (1987) has described a bias against the description of strategic or tactical choices in analysis. It is increasingly clear that some patients' fear of exposing shameful defectiveness underlies their resistance to entering analysis, as does the originally described fear of an uncontrolled regression. It is useful to delay interpretation until shame sensitivity can be assessed and modulated. In the past there was pressure to keep the preparatory therapy brief so that the analyst would not become too well known to the patient. Less concern about strict anonymity allows more time for the patient's resistances to abate before the recommendation is made. Methods are described and clinical illustrations show how a deepening process can be fostered, how indications of readiness for the transition can be assessed, and when and how the recommendation can be made.

[[RunningHead]]

The Singularity of Being: Lacan and the Immortal Within

Mari Ruti

Mari Ruti

170 St. George Street
Toronto, ON M5R 2MB
Canada
E-mail: mari.ruti@utoronto.ca

Mari Ruti   Associate Professor of Critical Theory, University of Toronto English Department.

Drawing on the work of Eric Santner, Slavoj Žižek, and Alenka Zupančič, this paper constructs a theory of subjective singularity from a Lacanian perspective. It argues that, unlike the “subject” (who comes into existence as a result of symbolic prohibition), or the “person” (who is aligned with the narcissistic conceits of the imaginary), the singular self emerges in response to a galvanizing directive arising from the real. This directive summons the individual to a “character” beyond his or her social and intersubjective investments. Consequently, singularity expresses the individual's nonnegotiable distinctiveness, eccentricity, or idiosyncrasy at the same time as it prevents both symbolic and imaginary closure. It opens to layers of rebelliousness that indicate that there are components of human life that exceed the realm of normative sociality. Indeed, insofar as singularity articulates something about the “undead” pulse of jouissance, it connects the individual to a paradoxical kind of immortality. This does not mean that the individual will not die, but rather that he or she is capable of “transcendent” experiences, such as heightened states of creativity, that (always momentarily) reach “outside” the parameters of mortal life. Such experiences allow the individual to feel “real” in ways that fend off symbolic abduction and psychic death.

[[RunningHead]]

Against Understanding: Why Understanding Should not Be Viewed as an Essential Aim of Psychoanalytic Treatment

Bruce Fink

Bruce Fink

914 Rockwell Hall
Pittsburgh, PA 15282

Bruce Fink   Professor of Psychology, Duquesne University; member, Ecole de la Cause Freudienne, Paris; affiliate member, Pittsburgh Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

The current emphasis on understanding in psychoanalysis—on the analysand's part as well as on the analyst's—is excessive if we assume that the most essential aim of psychoanalytic treatment is change. Situated within the Lacanian register or dimension of the imaginary, the process of understanding can be seen to reduce the unfamiliar to the familiar, to transform the radically other into the same, and to render the analyst hard of hearing. Our ability as analysts to detect the unconscious via slips of the tongue, slurred words, mixed metaphors, and the like is compromised by our emphasis on understanding and can be rectified only by taking as our fundamental premise that we do not understand what our analysands are saying. The emphasis on understanding can also do a disservice to analysands, who learn to observe themselves and to explain their feelings and behaviors to themselves and others in sophisticated terms without necessarily changing. But change can perfectly well occur in the absence of understanding, which in fact often impedes change.

[[RunningHead]]

The Missing Tombstone: Reflections on Mourning and Creativity

Anna Ornstein

Anna Ornstein

60 Longwood Avenue
Brookline, MA 02446
E-mail: a2727b@aol.com

Anna Ornstein   Lecturer in Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute.

There are differences as well as similarities between mourning occurring after the loss of a single individual under ordinary civilized conditions, and mourning after multiple losses under traumatic conditions. A brief review of the psychoanalytic theory of mourning articulated by Freud in 1917 and modified in the 1960s is followed by an equally brief review of the relationship between artistic creativity and mourning. Because survivors of major disasters who suffer multiple losses need time to recover before they can engage the emotionally demanding task of mourning, the mourning process in these cases is frequently delayed. “Memorial spaces” appear to facilitate delayed mourning because such spaces are responsive to a deeply felt need by survivors of major tragedies to articulate what they experience as unspeakable and unshareable. The memories and the pain associated with them are then welcome because mourning is not about forgetting; mourning is about remembering, a process that may take a lifetime.

[[RunningHead]]

The Lure of the Symptom in Psychoanalytic Treatment

Thomas Ogden and Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

306 Laurel Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

6655 Travis Street, Suite 500
Houston, TX 7703
E-mail: ggabbard12@aol.com

Glen Gabbard   Brown Foundation Chair and Professor of Psychiatry, Baylor College of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, Houston-Galveston Psychoanalytic Institute.

Psychoanalysis, which at its core is a search for truth, stands in a subversive position vis-à-vis the contemporary therapeutic culture that places a premium on symptomatic “cure.” Nevertheless, analysts are vulnerable to succumbing to the internal and external pressures for the achievement of symptomatic improvement. In this communication we trace the evolution of Freud's thinking about the relationship between the aims of psychoanalysis and the alleviation of symptoms. We note that analysts today may recapitulate Freud's early struggles in their pursuit of symptom removal. We present an account of a clinical consultation in which the analytic pair were ensnared in an impasse that involved the analyst's preoccupation with the intransigence of one of the patient's symptoms. We suggest alternative ways of working with these clinical issues and offer some thoughts on how our own work as analysts and consultants to colleagues has been influenced by our understanding of what frequently occurs when the analyst becomes symptom-focused.

[[RunningHead]]

An Order of Pure Decision: Growing up in a Virtual World and the Adolescent's Experience of Being-in-A-Body

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA
E-mail: alemma@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Alessandra Lemma   Trust-wide Head of Psychology, Tavistock and Portman NHS Foundation Trust; Visiting Professor, Psychoanalysis Unit, University College London; Visiting Professor of Psychological Therapies, School of Health and Human Sciences, Essex University; General Editor, New Library of Psychoanalysis; Editor, Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: Theory, Practice and Research.

Technological advances and the dominant values of contemporary culture make it possible and acceptable to alter, extend, or altogether bypass the body and its functions in actuality and in virtual space. This has contributed to a split between the body and the self, leading to a disembodied subjectivity that may encourage a neglect of the body's unconscious meaning for the individual. Due to the psychic requirement during adolescence to accommodate the reality of the changing body, some vulnerable adolescents are especially primed for the seductions of virtual space—a “space” that is nowadays not only culturally sanctioned, but also idealized. The use of cyberspace can become a psychic refuge from the challenge of integrating the reality and meaning of the sexual body into the image of the self. Two case examples illustrate how for some vulnerable adolescents it is through the use of cyberspace that confusion about the real body can be denied or disavowed; for them the integrity of the self is sustained through pseudorepresentations of the body defensively experienced in terms of “play” rather than pathology.

responseSet.authorCitation: Shahar, G., Porcerelli, J. H., Kamoo, R., Epperson, C. N., Czarkowski, K. A., Magriples, U. & Mayes, L. C.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Defensive Projection, Superimposed on Simplistic Object Relations, Erodes Patient-Provider Relationships in High-Risk Pregnancy: An Empirical Investigation

Golan Shahar , John Porcerelli , Ray Kamoo , C. Epperson , Kathryn Czarkowski , Urania Magriples and Linda Mayes

Linda Mayes

Beer-Sheva 84105
ISRAEL
E-mail: shaharg@bgu.ac.il

Linda Mayes   Professor, Yale Child Study Center.

In an attempt to illustrate the relevance of psychoanalytic theory and research to behavior medicine, an empirical investigation was conducted of females treated at a high-risk pregnancy specialty clinic (N = 58). Drawing from psychoanalytic object relations theory, it was hypothesized and confirmed that use of projection as a defense mechanism during pregnancy, superimposed on simplistic object relations, predicted an erosion of patient-provider relationships during the pregnancy/postdelivery period. Findings are interpreted through the perspective of mentalization, pertaining to individuals' ability to understand the mental states of self and others, specifically under significant stress. Implications for psychoanalytically oriented assessment and treatment, and for the rift between psychoanalysis and research, are discussed.

responseSet.title: Losing a Father All over Again: The Termination of an Analysis of an Adolescent Boy Suffering from Father Loss
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Losing a Father All over Again: The Termination of an Analysis of an Adolescent Boy Suffering from Father Loss

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

4180 La Jolla Village Drive, Suite 550B
La Jolla, CA 92037
E-mail: mumford2@cox.net

Alan Sugarman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Supervising Child and Adolescent Analyst, San Diego Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California, San Diego.

Criteria for beginning and conducting the termination phase of psychoanalysis have provoked debate and confusion from the early days of psychoanalysis. Gabbard (2009) has recently pointed to the field's tendency to cling to idealized versions of these criteria as a way to deal with disagreements. The situation becomes more complicated for child and adolescent psychoanalysts because their patients are in the midst of a developmental process at the very time they are engaged in a psychoanalytic process. The termination phase of an adolescent male suffering from father loss is presented in depth in order to provide clinical data toward further consideration of the vexing questions surrounding termination in psychoanalysis. His termination is used to examine the relative importance of losing the analyst as a transference object as against a developmental object; the meaning of action during termination; the complicating role of trauma vis-à-vis termination; and the importance of the post-termination phase of analysis. It is suggested that his termination phase demonstrates that a “good enough” termination involves the development of a self-analyzing capacity that continues to evolve and develop after termination.

[[RunningHead]]

Rediscovering Psychoanalysis: Thinking and Dreaming, Learning and Forgetting. By Thomas H. Ogden. London: Routledge/Institute of Psychoanalysis, 2009, 168 pp., $107.95 hardcover, $36.95 paperback.

Andreas Kraebber

Andreas Kraebber

876 Park Avenue, Suite 1C
New York, NY 10075
E-mail: akraebber@gmail.com

Andreas Kraebber  

As the title of this collection of papers suggests, Ogden's latest mission is to help us “rediscover” psychoanalysis. He borrows from Bion, here quoted from one of his seminars, to point us in the right direction: “Of

184

course, we believe as analysts—rightly or wrongly—that analysis is helpful. But that belief is liable to hide from us the extraordinary nature, the mystery of psychoanalysis. Such a lot of analysts seem to be bored with their subject; they have lost the capacity to wonder” (p. 84).

Mystery, wonder, awe: these are the experiences that should invigorate our work. Our sacred task, after all, is to facilitate the rebirth of the individual into a more complete form of human existence. But if this is Ogden's mission, his mission statement urges us to keep two aspects of our work distinct. We must not confuse method (our scientific craft and its reliance on shared ideology) and style (our hermeneutic art and its reliance on individualized history). Ogden's method elaborates what Spezzano (1996) has called the “three faces” of “two-person” psychoanalysis: that is, its developmental, ontological, and epistemological theories. But if much of the text of this book focuses on Ogden's psychoanalytic method (how we develop, how we come into being, how we know), the subtext focuses on his psychoanalytic style (how he uniquely does what he does to facilitate development, existence, and self-knowledge).

In this dichotomy between method and style lies a tension perhaps best captured by the conflict between two approaches to our work: the idiographic (highlighting the unique elements) and the nomothetic (highlighting general, lawlike statements). In this book (by my count his eighth), Ogden leans about as far as he ever has toward the idiographic pole (to the left, perhaps), emphasizing all that is irreducibly unique about the analyst, his or her style, the analysand, and that third entity, the analytic couple. There is something powerfully seductive about this approach (this method), and the simpler he puts it (the style he opts for here), the easier it goes down. And yet reading this mature Ogden leaves me with a nagging suspicion that interspersed between the idealized purity of his style—one “free of cliché, jargon, and ‘therapeutic’ and other ‘knowing’ tones of voice” (p. 3)—hides a significant sprinkling of eye-winking irony. However simple it appears, keeping method and style distinct while distilling both is anything but simple. It requires hour upon hour of hard work to laboriously reach the zen koan-like simplicity (one hand clapping and all) of the true “me and us” (third) moment Ogden espouses. The price for purity—if the distillate fails—is a nebulous mush.

Theory and Practice

Handbook of Attachment: Theory, Research, and Clinical Applications. 2nd ed. Edited by Jude Cassidy and Phillip R. Shaver. New York: Guilford Press, 2008, 1020 pp., $125.00.

Jill Miller

Jill Miller

3025 East Cedar Lane
Denver, CO 80209
E-mail: jmiller0442@mac.com

Jill Miller  

John Bowlby was an analyst and a psychiatrist, trained in the Freudian tradition of psychoanalysis. His research and clinical skills focused on young children and their families. His work was “enriched by fields such as ethology, cognitive psychology and systems theory,” whereas his preoccupations were with “the joys and sorrows, the hope and despair, incurred in the making, sustaining and breaking of affectional bonds” (Hamilton 1985, p. 2). To an extent Bowlby departed from the psychoanalysis of his time, as he was interested in a person's real experiences, as opposed to a singular focus on fantasy, and deviated from Freud's instinct theory because it did not explain the behavior he saw as he observed young children; yet his frame of reference was always psychoanalysis. In his 1979 paper “Psychoanalysis as Art and Science” he fully understood the tension between therapy and research, as each requires a different focus of study, mode of acquiring information, and mental outlook, and is judged on criteria of its own.

Based on biology and evolutionary theory, attachment theory is premised on the idea that the survival of human beings depends on the ability to establish and maintain emotional ties to another. There are four basic assumptions underlying Bowlby's theories (Steele 2003): (1) the intimate emotional bonds individuals have with one another have a primary status and a biological function; (2) how children are treated has a profound influence on their development and personality formation; (3) attachment behavior is part of an organizational system of internal working models that guide individuals in their relational lives; (4) while attachment behavior is resistant to change, it is not impermeable to outside influences.

With his attachment theory Bowlby changed the way parent-child relationships are viewed; together with his colleagues, he offered a way to study these relationships with a scientifically sound research methodology.

Primary Process and Research

Primary Process Thinking: Theory, Measurement, and Research (Volume 1 and 2). Robert R. Holt. Lanham, MD: Jason Aronson, 2009, 153 pp. + CD, $60.00.

J. Fowler

J. Fowler

25 Main Street
Stockbridge, MA 01262
E-mail: christopher.fowler@austenriggs.net

J. Fowler  

In recent decades, critics of psychoanalysis have pointed to the guild's general disregard for empirical investigations of the theory's fundamental tenets. While these diatribes bear a likeness to practitioners who consider psychoanalysis too complex to be examined through reductionistic empirical methods, notable exceptions include the brilliant contributions of Eric Kandel, Robert Wallerstein, Sidney Blatt, Lester Luborsky, and Otto Kernberg. With the long-awaited publication of this book, we can now formally add Robert R. Holt to the list of foundational psychoanalytic theorists who not only develop and critique the theories of psychoanalysis, but who distill core principles into measurable, testable hypotheses.

Those who studied with Holt at New York University's Research Center for Mental Health (including Morris Eagle, Leo Goldberger, Nancy Goldberger, Fred Pine, Donald Spence, Paul Wachtel, Hartvig Dahl, Merton M. Gill, and Robert J. Langs) are intimately aware of the scope of his psychoanalytic research into areas of subliminal influences on cognition, measuring primary process, studying cognitive styles, sensory deprivation, and dreaming, Others may be familiar with Holt's earlier works, including Freud Reappraised (1989) and fourteen other books or edited volumes, while still others may know of his humanitarian efforts as the founding director of the Program on Peace and Global Policy Studies at NYU. In this two-volume set (Volume 1 presents in three chapters the theoretical basis of primary process ideation; Volume 2, a CD-ROM containing eighteen chapters, includes an exhaustive scoring manual, research review, and normative database), Holt develops not only a comprehensive theory of mental operations but a system for analyzing their products using his concepts of primary process.

Primary Process Thinking: Theory, Measurement, and Research is the culmination of over fifty years of diligent work by Holt and his group.

587

Introduction: Not for Child Analysts' Eyes Only

Lee Ascherman

Lee Ascherman

E-mail: lascherman@uab.edu

Lee Ascherman  

While JAPA's Child Analysis and Development Section provides an important home to child analysts, the papers in this issue have much to offer to all readers. Certainly our understanding of our adult analysands is informed by their internal and external childhood experiences, whether communicated directly or through transference. Erikson (1950) expanded our ideas about human development beyond traditional notions, highlighting the developmental challenges of later ages. And any of us who have conducted analytic work with older adults have experienced firsthand the vibrancy of early developmental experiences throughout the life span. Most important, the premise and promise of analytic work with adults relies on the conviction that the outcome of the developmental process is not so crystallized as to be refractory to change catalyzed by revisiting forces from childhood through the transference.

At first glance, the three articles in this section would seem to have little in common. One focuses on termination in the context of father loss. Another addresses the world of cyberspace and its relevance to adolescent development. The third discusses the analyst's sexual orientation in relation to adolescent transference. However, on closer examination, there is tremendous overlap in their efforts to address questions critically important for contemporary analytic thought and practice. All use rich clinical examples to illustrate their ideas about development, transference and countertransference, body image and sexuality, neutrality, abstinence, self-revelation, and termination.

In “Losing a Father All Over Again; The Termination of an Analysis of an Adolescent Boy Suffering from Father Loss,” Alan Sugarman provides us an important addition to the relatively small literature on termination and the even smaller literature on termination with children and adolescents. Guide to Psychoanalytic Developmental Theories. By Joseph Palombo, Harold K. Bendicsen, and Barry J. Koch. New York: Springer, 2009, xlviii + 416 pp., $119.00.

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

122 South Michigan Avenue #1307
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

A basic tenet of psychoanalysis is that early experiences in a child's life will have an enduring effect on the adult he or she becomes. Consequently, it is commonplace to read that every psychoanalytic theory includes some mention of development, whether implicit in its conceptual categories or fully elaborated as a developmental theory in its own right. Anyone wishing to teach psychoanalytic theory to the next generation of scholars and clinicians faces a difficult decision, not only in deciding which theories to include but also in addressing their implicit or explicit perspectives on development.

Such was the task facing Joseph Palombo, Harold Bendicsen, and Barry Koch, three educators who share a clinical background in social work and extensive experience in training programs (including, for Palombo and Bendicsen, the Child and Adolescent Psychoanalytic Therapy Program at the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis). In their teaching they found that students' “unfamiliarity with the history of the psychoanalytic movement limited their ability to appreciate the context in which the creative ferment of the first half of the twentieth century occurred” (p. vii). Guide to Psychoanalytic Developmental Theories is their remedy for that lack, and the rest of us are the grateful beneficiaries of the care and thoughtfulness with which they have satisfied their pedagogical desire.

The book comprises seven sections: Drive Theory; Ego Psychological Theories; Object Relational Theories; Life Cycle Theory; Interpersonal Theory; Theories of the Self; and Attachment Theories (Part 1: Traditional Attachment Theories; Part 2: Neurodevelomental Attachment Theories).

1041

responseSet.title: Rereading Masud Khan Today: Have His Writings Fallen with Him?
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rereading Masud Khan Today: Have His Writings Fallen with Him?

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen

451 West End Avenue, Suite 1J
New York, NY 10024-5359
E-mail: sjcoenmd@aol.com

Stanley Coen  

Once upon a time, in the 1970s and early 1980s, many of us thought of Masud Khan as the most interesting, helpful psychoanalytic writer on schizoid states and perversions, and even more broadly on the treatment of very difficult patients. Teaching perversion in the second-year course on psychopathology at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, I especially wanted candidates to read articles by Khan (1962, 1964, 1965a, b, 1969) that seemed to me to best capture and evoke how it feels to work with perverse patients. I drew on Khan's ideas in my own writing. I have found them very helpful, and have admired Khan as a clear and evocative writer.

Younger psychoanalysts may now know little about Masud Khan except for his notoriety, especially after the publication of three biographies (Cooper 1993; Willoughby 2005; Hopkins 2006). He comes across as unable to contain his narcissistic neediness, arrogantly misusing others—patients, colleagues, wives, lovers, friends—for his own needs, destructive toward himself and others, violent, vulnerable, unpredictable, unreliable, untrustworthy. Charming and charismatic, worldly, smart, literate, and exotic, he quickly captivated the psychoanalytic world, only to crash and burn when he provoked and enraged the British psychoanalytic establishment with his boundary crossing and boundary violations with his patients, and with his alcoholism, extravagant storytelling and lying, and other outrageous behavior. Before his fall, he published three extraordinary books and many articles, served as editor of the International Psychoanalytical Library and as associate editor of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, edited and helped write a number of Donald Winnicott's papers, and in general had become a luminary in the psychoanalytic world. Afterward, his status as a training analyst and a member of the British Psychoanalytical Society and his membership in the International Psychoanalytical Association were revoked.

Thinking for Clinicians: Philosophical Resources for Contemporary Psychoanalysis and the Humanistic Psychotherapies. By Donna M. Orange. New York: Routledge, 2010, 168 pp., $29.99 paperback.

Robert White

Robert White

291 Whitney Avenue
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: robertswhite@comcast.net

Robert White  

The stated purpose of this book is to provide the psychoanalytic clinician with “tools and context for thinking about psychotherapeutic work and theories” (p. 1). The tools in this case are philosophical; the aim is to feel “more at home and competent in the world of ideas” (p. 2). The author's emphasis is to use philosophical ideas to improve clinical work. She sees philosophy as an antidote to the frequent psychoanalytic tendency to get caught up in schools and exclude other forms of thinking. We fail to question our basic assumptions and operating principles. Her attitude is one of fallibilism, the doctrine that beliefs do not need to be grounded in certainty. She would hold theory lightly, as well as “any particular view of meaning in the patient's experience” (p. 6). Her guiding principle is that of a hermeneutic spirit. This would include a sensitivity to dialogue between therapist and patient, each grounded in their particular worlds and cultures. These are all reasonable attitudes.

809

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Growing Old: A Journey of Self-Discovery. By Danielle Quinodoz. Translated by David Alcorn. New York: Routledge, 2009, xiv + 218 pp., $27.50 paperback.

Stanley Leavy

Stanley Leavy

200 Leeder Hill Drive
Hamden, CT 06517-2758
E-mail: stanleavy@sbcglobal.net

Stanley Leavy  

One might suppose that in the remote past, when it was a scarcer commodity than it is now, old age held a higher intrinsic value. Survival into one's eighties, say, despite disease, predators, tribal enemies, and hungry heirs, would have conferred a mystic dignity—decline in productivity, fighting power, and fertility notwithstanding. Contrariwise, I read recently that over half the babies born in 2010—in favored nations—can expect to live beyond one hundred years; old age will become a commonplace. To an extent it already is, and the consequences are reflected in the ever growing literature on old age, including the psychological. In the background, a note of ambivalence can be heard. While everyone wants to live a long life, to be old, or almost worse, to look old, is undesirable. When, therefore, we pick up a book dealing with longevity in any aspect, we are entitled to sniff for hints of anxiety or condescension. Words of praise for what an old man or woman has achieved may betray the writer's reluctance to recognize that he or she too has this fate in store. One might as an old person wonder whether any of the young (meaning the not-yet-old) ever contemplate what it might really feel like to be old.

So it is with uncommon pleasure that I can say of this book by Danielle Quinodoz, a member of the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society, a teacher and consultant at a gerontological hospital with wide clinical experience, and herself a grandmother, that this time my suspicions turned out to be unfounded. The title turns out to be its theme, in a more literal sense than is usual for titles. It is a book about growing old, a process of what Erikson called “epigenesis,” in which hitherto latent possibilities may emerge, or when they don't, call for treatment. In this the modern economic notion of “growth” as expansion plays no part, though this book's amusing cover illustration of (internalized?) Russian dolls might suggest it. In many ways old age is really a contraction, with the physical and mental limitations it imposes, but, often surprisingly, new attributes and activities may make all the difference—as, in a poem by A. D. Hope, the coming of old age is likened to the display of the New England forest colors in autumn, hitherto suppressed by the chlorophyll of summer.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Selectively Questioning Orthodoxies: Commentary on Wilson

Deborah Cabaniss and Hillery Bosworth

Hillery Bosworth

903 Park Avenue
New Yark, NY 10075
E-mail:dsc3@columbia.edu

Hillery Bosworth   Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, NYU School of Medicine; faculty, Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Questioning orthodoxy in psychoanalysis has a long tradition and often leads to needed change. Consider Freud's reevaluation of the topographic model (1923), Kohut's reconsideration of oedipally focused psychoanalytic technique (1979), and Kernberg's recasting of the concept of countertransference (1965). In this vein, reconsidering the many facets of the training analysis is a worthy theoretical endeavor. Hardly a day goes by that the current system of training analysis in the United States isn't the focus of a lengthy diatribe on the American Psychoanalytic Association's online discussion page. Much of the discussion revolves around the question of whether the analysis of candidates should be conducted only by a specific cohort of vetted training analysts, or at least about how the vetting should be conducted. Myriad voices with diverse interests have engaged in this discussion, many of which are concerned about power at the level of the institute and within the analytic dyad.

Mitchell Wilson, whose “Putting Practice into Theory: Making the Training Analysis System Coherent” appears in this issue of JAPA, is such a voice. His call for the abolishment of the existing training analyst system begins with the premise that the training analysis should be considered solely therapeutic, not educational. He asserts that the literature supports the view that the best practice for a therapeutic analysis is one in which the analyst maintains a decentered, nonauthoritarian stance. Since he believes that the existing system can give the training analyst special status and authority, Wilson concludes that this system undermines the training analyst's ability to maintain what he considers to be this proper stance. Wilson recalls the warnings of other authors (Racker 1957; Kernberg 1996; Silber 1996)

[[RunningHead]]

Partners in Thought: Working with Unformulated Experience, Dissociation, and Enactment. By Donnel B. Stern. New York: Routledge, 2009, xx + 229 pp., $90.00 hardcover, $34.95 paperback.

Karin Ahbel-Rappe

Karin Ahbel-Rappe

304 1/2 South State Street
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: k.ahbel-rappe@sbcglobal.net

Karin Ahbel-Rappe  

Donnel Stern, to highlight what is well known, is one of the founding, significant voices of relational psychoanalysis, that movement in psychoanalysis that organizes itself around the recognition that what happens in an analysis—what is possible, what is ruled out, what goes well, what goes wrong, what is said, what remains implicit—is a function of the relationship between analyst and analysand. Stern's 2003 monograph, Unformulated Experience, is bound to be a classic in the relational literature. His new book, Partners in Thought, gathers together a subsequent set of papers. It carries forward from the earlier work the elaboration of the relational model and the core idea of unconscious experience as unformulated experience. What it adds is the theme of the relationship between dissociation and enactment. As a set of essays, the book does not develop an argument gradually over the course of its chapters but is instead, as its author describes it, “a collection of various approaches to the relationship of dissociation and enactment” (p. xiii).

The sort of bare-bones theoretical argument that can be gleaned from the book is as follows: The unconscious is not constituted by formed and then repressed meanings. Rather, the unconscious is unformulated experience; unformulated experience is dissociated experience; dissociated experience is potential experience, experience that has not been actualized, that has never been symbolized. Certain sectors of a particular person's experience may be strongly defensively dissociated. These are “not-me,” in the idiom Stern adopts from Harry Stack Sullivan. Not-me is what a subject cannot tolerate, cannot bear to find in him- or herself. The threat is such that the subject refuses to allow formulation of these possibilities. When what is dissociated threatens to become conscious, the result, in treatment (and out, one assumes), is enactment. Enactment is the interpersonalization of dissociation (p. 14). It functions to insist, “It is not that I am x” by insisting that “It is that you are y,” where x and y are somehow complementary. In a mutual enactment, analyst and patient are reciprocally engaged in versions of this.

responseSet.title: Incest Avoidance: Oedipal and Preoedipal, Natural and Cultural
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Incest Avoidance: Oedipal and Preoedipal, Natural and Cultural

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

412 South Callaway Center
Atlanta, GA 30322
E-mail: rpaul@emory.edu

Robert Paul  

Why do most people experience a subjective aversion to the idea of incestuous sexual relations? To help answer this question, recent strands of thinking in both cultural and evolutionary anthropology are considered together with psychoanalytic theories regarding incest avoidance. Coevolutionary theories that propose ways to think about genetic and cultural inheritance as partially independent of each other, evolutionary arguments about the reproductive advantages of incest avoidance, structuralist theory arguing for a kind of incest aversion unrelated to any possible Darwinian selective advantage, and other trends in biosocial research into the origins of the incest avoidance are considered. Finally, a synthesis is proposed that seeks to expand our understanding of the oedipal and preoedipal dynamics of the aversion as these are conceptualized in psychoanalytic theory.
